Gallabox.history: Gallabox is an Indian WhatsApp Business API platform founded in 2020. It provides businesses tools to manage WhatsApp conversations, build chatbots, and run marketing campaigns. The startup received venture backing and targets SMBs in India and Southeast Asia.
Gallabox.summary_abuse: Gallabox is a WhatsApp Business API platform. WhatsApp-based messaging tools carry risk of being used for spam and bulk messaging campaigns. While Gallabox is a legitimate Meta Business Partner, WhatsApp messaging automation tools are frequently used for unsolicited marketing and scam messages in Indian markets.

Gallery.history: Gallery is an open-source PHP photo album and image gallery application first released in 2000 by Bharat Mediratta. It was one of the most popular web gallery applications in the early 2000s. Gallery 3 was the last major version. Development has effectively stopped, with no significant commits since around 2015, making it effectively abandoned.
Gallery.summary_abuse: Gallery is an abandoned open-source PHP photo gallery application. Old installations with unpatched vulnerabilities have historically been targeted for server compromise and malware injection. The project's abandonment means no security updates, creating risk for sites still running it.

Gambio.history: Gambio is a German e-commerce software company founded in 2004 in Bremen. It developed a popular e-commerce platform based on modified osCommerce code, targeting German-speaking small and medium businesses. The company has grown to serve over 25,000 online shops and remains independently owned.
Gambio.summary_abuse: Gambio is a German e-commerce platform used by legitimate retailers. As a hosted e-commerce solution, it can be used to operate counterfeit goods or fraudulent shops, but Gambio has standard AUP enforcement. No major platform-level abuse incidents have been documented.

Gameball.history: Gameball is an Egyptian loyalty and gamification platform founded in 2018 in Cairo. It provides loyalty programs, referral systems, and gamification tools for e-commerce and SaaS businesses. The company has raised venture funding and serves businesses in the MENA region and globally.
Gameball.summary_abuse: Gameball is a loyalty and gamification SaaS platform with no documented abuse history. As a B2B tool for customer engagement, it presents low consumer-facing abuse risk.

Gamma.history: Gamma is an AI-powered presentation and document creation tool founded in 2020 by Grant Lee, Jon Noronha, and James Fox in San Francisco. The company raised significant venture funding and launched its AI presentation tool publicly in 2023, rapidly gaining millions of users. It became one of the most popular AI-powered alternatives to PowerPoint and Google Slides.
Gamma.summary_abuse: Gamma is an AI presentation tool that has grown rapidly. As a content creation platform, it could be used to create misleading or spam presentations, but no major documented abuse incidents are known. AI-generated content can be misused for disinformation, and Gamma presentations can be published publicly.

Gamooga.history: Gamooga is an Indian marketing automation and customer engagement platform founded around 2012, targeting e-commerce companies. It provided behavioral targeting, push notifications, and email automation. The company appears to have gone dormant with limited activity in recent years.
Gamooga.summary_abuse: Gamooga is a marketing automation platform that appears dormant. Marketing automation tools carry inherent risk of being used for spam or intrusive behavioral tracking. No specific documented abuse incidents are known for Gamooga.

GaniPara.history: GaniPara appears to be a Turkish e-commerce or marketplace platform. Limited public information is available about the company's founding or history.
GaniPara.summary_abuse: GaniPara is a niche Turkish marketplace platform with limited public information. As a marketplace, standard e-commerce abuse risks apply including counterfeit goods. No specific documented abuse incidents are known.

Gannett CMS.history: Gannett CMS is the content management system used internally by Gannett Co., the largest newspaper publisher in the United States, owning USA Today and hundreds of local papers. Gannett developed proprietary CMS technology to manage publishing across its large network of publications. Gannett itself was founded in 1906 and went public decades later.
Gannett CMS.summary_abuse: Gannett CMS is a proprietary publishing platform used internally by Gannett newspapers. As an internal enterprise tool not available to external parties, direct abuse risk is minimal. Gannett's news properties have been noted for heavy advertising tracker loads on their consumer-facing websites.

Gastronaut.history: Gastronaut is a German restaurant technology platform founded around 2019, providing table reservation, online ordering, and digital menu management systems for restaurants. No notable acquisitions or major controversy events are documented.
Gastronaut.summary_abuse: Gastronaut is a restaurant management SaaS with no documented abuse history. As a B2B restaurant tool, it has minimal consumer-facing abuse surface.

Gator.history: Gator is HostGator's drag-and-drop website builder, launched around 2017. HostGator was acquired by Endurance International Group (later Newfold Digital) in 2012. Gator is a no-code website builder competing with Wix and Squarespace, targeting small businesses and individuals.
Gator.summary_abuse: Gator is HostGator's website builder. HostGator hosting infrastructure has historically been used to host spam and phishing sites due to its low-cost plans. Gator-built sites can be used for scam or counterfeit storefronts. HostGator/Newfold Digital has abuse reporting mechanisms but response times have been criticized.

Gatsby.history: Gatsby was created by Kyle Mathews and first released in 2015 as an open-source React-based static site generator. It grew into one of the most popular Jamstack frameworks, with Gatsby Inc. raising $15M in VC funding. In 2023, Netlify acquired Gatsby Inc., merging its cloud services with Netlify's platform. The open-source framework continues under MIT license.
Gatsby.summary_abuse: Gatsby is a widely-used open-source React framework for building static sites with no notable abuse history. As a developer tool, it has no consumer-facing surface and is used primarily for legitimate website development.

Gatsby Cloud Image CDN.history: Gatsby Cloud Image CDN is a cloud image optimization and CDN service released by Gatsby Inc. as part of Gatsby Cloud around 2021. After Netlify's acquisition of Gatsby Inc. in 2023, it became part of Netlify's offerings. It provides automatic image optimization and delivery for Gatsby sites.
Gatsby Cloud Image CDN.summary_abuse: Gatsby Cloud Image CDN is an image optimization service with no documented abuse history. As an infrastructure service for Gatsby developers, it has no direct consumer-facing surface that would enable abuse.

Gauges.history: Gauges (Gaug.es) is a simple, privacy-focused web analytics tool launched around 2011 by GitHub co-founder Scott Chacon and team at GitHub as a side project. It was later spun off independently. Gauges focuses on real-time analytics without complex tracking, positioning itself as a lightweight Google Analytics alternative.
Gauges.summary_abuse: Gauges is a privacy-focused web analytics tool with minimal data collection by design. No documented abuse history. As a simple website traffic counter, abuse potential is low.

GBooking.history: GBooking is a Russian online booking and scheduling SaaS platform founded around 2012, targeting service businesses such as salons, medical clinics, and fitness studios. It offers appointment booking widgets and CRM features. The company is primarily active in the Russian market.
GBooking.summary_abuse: GBooking is an appointment scheduling platform for service businesses. No documented abuse incidents are known. Standard booking platform risks apply such as fake appointments or spam through the booking form.

Gcore.history: Gcore (formerly G-Core Labs) is a global edge network and cloud infrastructure provider founded in 2014 in Luxembourg. Offers CDN, DDoS protection, cloud hosting, gaming infrastructure, and streaming services with 140+ PoPs worldwide.
Gcore.summary_abuse: Minimal consumer abuse risk as a B2B infrastructure provider. Services target enterprise clients. No dark patterns or deceptive practices noted. Standard infrastructure SLA-based service.

GDPR Cookie Consent Plugin by Webtoffee.history: The GDPR Cookie Consent plugin by WebToffee (formerly CookieLawInfo) was first released on WordPress.org around 2012, with WebToffee taking it over and rebranding it. It became one of the most popular GDPR compliance plugins for WordPress, accumulating over 1 million active installations. WebToffee is an Indian WordPress plugin development company.
GDPR Cookie Consent Plugin by Webtoffee.summary_abuse: GDPR Cookie Consent Plugin by Webtoffee is a WordPress compliance plugin with no documented abuse history. It is used to display cookie consent banners and manage user consents. Its purpose is privacy-enhancing. Some instances of misconfigured consent banners using dark patterns have been noted in the broader cookie banner ecosystem, but these are user configuration issues rather than vendor-level abuse.

GeeTest.history: GeeTest is a Chinese CAPTCHA and bot detection company founded in 2011 in Wuhan. It pioneered slide-puzzle CAPTCHA and behavioral-based bot detection as an alternative to Google reCAPTCHA. It became widely adopted in China and has expanded globally, serving financial services, gaming, and e-commerce sectors.
GeeTest.summary_abuse: GeeTest is a CAPTCHA and bot detection service used to protect websites from automated attacks. As a Chinese-origin security service, some privacy advocates have raised concerns about data collection and data transfer to Chinese servers. The service itself is anti-abuse in purpose and has no significant documented abuse incidents.

Geggio.history: Geggio is a Netherlands-based all-in-one funnel tool for SMBs combining landing pages, email, automations, and CRM; limited public history available.
Geggio.summary_abuse: Geggio is a small niche marketing funnel SaaS with no notable abuse signal documented. It does not appear to offer anonymous signups or arbitrary content hosting.

Gekosale.history: Gekosale is a Polish open-source ecommerce platform, originally the largest such platform in Poland. Divante, a Polish ecommerce agency, invested in Gekosale and took a 51% stake to develop an enterprise version 2.0. The platform appears to have declined in activity and is no longer prominently maintained.
Gekosale.summary_abuse: Gekosale is a self-hosted open-source ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. It has no consumer-facing hosted service and no known misuse patterns.

Gelato.history: Gelato is a global print-on-demand and fulfillment platform founded in 2007 in Oslo, Norway. Connects sellers to local print partners in 32+ countries. Integrates with Shopify, Etsy, WooCommerce.
Gelato.summary_abuse: Legitimate B2B print-on-demand platform with transparent pricing. Standard freemium model with pay-per-order.

Gemius.history: Gemius is a European internet research and web analytics company founded in 1999 in Warsaw, Poland. Provides audience measurement, ad research, and digital analytics primarily to Central and Eastern European markets.
Gemius.summary_abuse: Analytics company with audience measurement. Standard data collection for analytics. GDPR compliant. No dark patterns.

GemPages.history: GemPages is a Shopify page builder app launched around 2018. Provides drag-and-drop landing page and product page creation for Shopify merchants with 65,000+ active installs.
GemPages.summary_abuse: Standard Shopify page builder with no notable abuse patterns. Subscription with clear pricing tiers.

GenerateBlocks.history: GenerateBlocks is a lightweight WordPress Gutenberg block plugin created by Tom Usborne, also behind GeneratePress. Launched around 2020, it provides core blocks for building layouts without page builder bloat.
GenerateBlocks.summary_abuse: Open-source WordPress plugin from a reputable developer. No abuse patterns. Has a premium paid version but no dark patterns.

GeneratePress.history: GeneratePress is a lightweight WordPress theme created by Tom Usborne. First released in 2014, emphasizes performance and accessibility. Has a free version on WordPress.org and paid premium plugin.
GeneratePress.summary_abuse: Reputable independent WordPress theme developer. No abuse patterns or dark practices. Transparent pricing with lifetime license option.

GeneratePress GP Premium.history: GeneratePress GP Premium is the paid add-on plugin for the GeneratePress WordPress theme, created by Tom Usborne. It provides additional design controls, site library, WooCommerce integration, and advanced customization options.
GeneratePress GP Premium.summary_abuse: Transparent one-time or annual license with clear terms. No dark patterns. Reputable independent developer. No aggressive upselling or deceptive renewal practices.

Genesis blocks.history: Genesis Blocks is a collection of Gutenberg blocks for WordPress, originally created as Atomic Blocks, later rebranded when acquired by StudioPress/WP Engine. Provides pre-built sections and page layouts.
Genesis blocks.summary_abuse: Open-source WordPress blocks plugin from WP Engine/StudioPress. No abuse patterns. Free with professional backing.

Genesis theme.history: Genesis is a WordPress theme framework created by Brian Gardner and sold through StudioPress, now owned by WP Engine. Launched in 2010, it became one of the most popular WordPress theme frameworks with child themes ecosystem.
Genesis theme.summary_abuse: Highly reputable WordPress theme framework. No abuse patterns. Now part of WP Engine ecosystem with continued development.

Genesys Chat.history: Genesys Chat is the chat component of the Genesys Cloud CX contact center platform, a leading enterprise customer experience solution. Genesys was founded in 1990 in San Francisco and provides omnichannel customer service tools to large enterprises.
Genesys Chat.summary_abuse: Enterprise contact center software from a well-established vendor. Standard B2B security practices. No consumer-facing dark patterns.

Genesys Cloud.history: Genesys Cloud CX is a cloud-based contact center and customer experience platform by Genesys, founded in 1990. It provides AI-driven omnichannel routing, workforce management, analytics, and digital engagement for enterprise call centers.
Genesys Cloud.summary_abuse: Enterprise contact center platform from a major vendor. Strong security certifications. No consumer-facing dark patterns. B2B enterprise focus.

GeneXus.history: GeneXus is a low-code development platform founded in 1988 in Montevideo, Uruguay by Artech. It generates applications in multiple languages from a single model. Used in Latin America, Japan, and globally for enterprise application development.
GeneXus.summary_abuse: Long-established enterprise low-code platform with no abuse patterns. B2B focus with standard licensing. Strong presence in Latin America and Japan.

genezio.history: Genezio is a cloud platform for deploying backend applications, created around 2022 in Romania. Limited founding details are publicly available.
genezio.summary_abuse: Genezio is a developer deployment platform with no notable abuse signal. It is aimed at developers and does not expose consumer-facing content-hosting features.

Genially.history: Genially is an online tool for creating interactive and animated content, including presentations, infographics, and educational materials. Founded in 2015 in Córdoba, Spain. Used widely in education and corporate training.
Genially.summary_abuse: Freemium interactive content platform with no notable abuse patterns. Transparent pricing tiers. Some aggressive upgrade prompts but within normal bounds.

Geniee.history: Geniee is a Japanese digital advertising and marketing technology company founded in 2010. It provides programmatic advertising, SSP (supply-side platform), DMP, and marketing automation tools primarily for the Japanese market.
Geniee.summary_abuse: Japanese ad tech company operating SSP and programmatic advertising. Moderate tracker density as core business is advertising. Operates within Japanese privacy regulations.

Geniee Chat.history: Geniee Chat is the customer engagement and live chat product offered by Geniee, a Japanese marketing technology company. Provides customer support chat and engagement tools for Japanese businesses.
Geniee Chat.summary_abuse: Customer chat product from Japanese ad tech company. Standard chat data collection. Privacy practices aligned with Japanese laws and parent company policies.

GenieWords.history: GenieWords is an AI-powered content writing platform. It provides tools for generating marketing copy, blog posts, and other written content using AI assistance.
GenieWords.summary_abuse: AI writing tool with standard SaaS patterns. No notable dark patterns though some aggressive upsell. Standard data collection.

Genoo.history: Genoo is a marketing automation and CRM platform designed for small to mid-sized businesses. Founded around 2008 in the US, it provides email marketing, landing pages, and lead management tools.
Genoo.summary_abuse: Small marketing automation platform with no notable abuse patterns. Standard email marketing practices. Clear pricing and terms.

Gentoo.history: Gentoo Linux was created by Daniel Robbins and first released in 2000 as a source-based Linux distribution inspired by the Ports system from BSD. Robbins donated the project to the Gentoo Foundation in 2004. The distribution remains actively maintained by a community of volunteers.
Gentoo.summary_abuse: Gentoo Linux has no notable abuse signal. It is a source-based Linux distribution used by technical power users and developers who want maximum control over their system configuration. No consumer-facing surface is present that fraudsters would target.

Geo Targetly.history: Geo Targetly is a geolocation-based website personalization service that redirects visitors or shows content based on their location. Used for content localization, compliance (GDPR), and marketing.
Geo Targetly.summary_abuse: Geo-targeting service primarily for compliance and personalization. Handles IP-based geolocation data. GDPR-focused use case for many customers. No deceptive patterns.

Geoapify.history: Geoapify is a location data and mapping API provider offering geocoding, routing, map tiles, and place data services. Founded around 2019 in Germany, it serves developers building location-based applications.
Geoapify.summary_abuse: Developer-focused mapping API with GDPR-compliant data processing in Europe. Transparent pay-as-you-go pricing. No dark patterns.

Geobytes.history: Geobytes was incorporated in Delaware, USA in 1999, founded by Adrian McElligott to build one of the first global IP address location databases by overlaying geographic user data on internet infrastructure maps. Originally serving the online advertising industry, it diversified after the dot-com bust into fraud prevention, site analytics, and geographic customization. Executive and R&D operations are based in Brisbane, Australia with US servers in Dallas/Fort Worth.
Geobytes.summary_abuse: Geobytes provides IP geolocation data and anti-spam software. Its free-tier IP locator API is occasionally used by threat actors for reconnaissance or geo-bypass detection, which is a low-level inherent risk for any geolocation service. The company itself has no documented role in enabling abuse. Its anti-spam tools are explicitly designed to counter abuse.

GEODIS.history: GEODIS is a French logistics and supply chain company with origins dating to 1904. It is a wholly-owned subsidiary of SNCF, the French state railway. GEODIS grew through multiple acquisitions including TNT Logistics (2008), OHL (2015), and Gantrex. It operates in over 160 countries and is one of Europe's largest logistics companies.
GEODIS.summary_abuse: GEODIS is a major global logistics company. Its digital tracking and logistics platforms are enterprise B2B tools with no notable consumer-facing abuse surface. Phishing emails impersonating logistics companies are a common attack vector industry-wide, but this is an external threat rather than a platform-level abuse issue.

Geonetric.history: Geonetric was founded in 1999 by Eric Engelmann as a website and application development company. In 2001 the company pivoted to focus exclusively on healthcare, after building a prototype for a major health system. Over the past 25 years it evolved into a full-service digital healthcare marketing agency trusted by health systems across the US. No major ownership changes or acquisition events are on record.
Geonetric.summary_abuse: Geonetric is a B2B healthcare marketing agency with a narrow enterprise client base. No documented abuse cases or fraud patterns associated with the platform. Its specialized healthcare niche and enterprise sales model provide very limited attack surface for consumer-facing abuse.

GEOvendas.history: GEOvendas is a Brazilian B2B sales management and geolocation platform for field sales teams, founded around 2010. It provides route planning, customer management, and sales tracking for commercial representatives in Brazil. No notable acquisitions or controversies are documented.
GEOvendas.summary_abuse: GEOvendas is a Brazilian B2B sales management SaaS with no documented abuse history. It is a niche tool for field sales teams and has minimal consumer-facing surface that would attract abuse.

Gerrit.history: Gerrit is an open-source web-based code review tool for Git repositories, originally developed at Google for the Android project and open-sourced in 2008. Widely used in large engineering organizations for code review workflows.
Gerrit.summary_abuse: Mature open-source code review tool maintained by Google and community. No abuse patterns. Standard self-hosted code review infrastructure.

Gesio.history: Gesio is a Spanish ERP and business management platform designed for retail and wholesale businesses. Provides inventory management, POS integration, and accounting features for SMBs.
Gesio.summary_abuse: Spanish business management SaaS targeting SMBs. No notable abuse patterns. Standard ERP features with transparent pricing.

Gestim.history: Gestim is a French property management software solution for real estate professionals. Provides tools for managing rental properties, tenants, and accounting for real estate agencies.
Gestim.summary_abuse: French property management software with no notable abuse patterns. B2B real estate tool with standard privacy practices.

Get Satisfaction.history: Get Satisfaction was a customer community and support platform founded in 2007 in San Francisco. It allowed companies to create online communities for customer feedback. Acquired by Sprinklr in 2015 and subsequently discontinued.
Get Satisfaction.summary_abuse: Discontinued customer community platform. Was acquired and phased out by Sprinklr. No active service to assess for abuse patterns.

Get Siimple.history: Siimple (Get Siimple) is a minimal CSS framework by Josemi Juanes. Provides flat and clean UI components with minimal styling. Open-source on GitHub.
Get Siimple.summary_abuse: Open-source CSS framework with no commercial interests or abuse patterns. Completely free library with no tracking or dark patterns.

GetAuto.history: GetAuto is a digital marketing and website platform for automotive dealerships. Provides dealer websites, inventory management, and lead generation tools for car dealerships.
GetAuto.summary_abuse: Automotive dealership platform with standard B2B SaaS practices. No notable consumer-facing abuse. Lead generation focused.

Getback.history: Getback is a CRM and customer communication platform for automotive dealerships. Provides tools for managing leads, customer follow-ups, and communications for car dealers.
Getback.summary_abuse: Automotive CRM with standard B2B practices. No notable abuse patterns. Niche vertical SaaS.

GetButton.history: GetButton.io is a simple chat button widget service that adds WhatsApp, Messenger, and other chat buttons to websites. Founded around 2018, provides a quick way for businesses to add chat functionality.
GetButton.summary_abuse: Simple chat widget with minimal data collection. Small tool with straightforward pricing. No dark patterns reported.

GetChat.history: GetChat is a multi-agent WhatsApp business platform that enables teams to manage WhatsApp communications from a shared inbox. Provides CRM integration and team collaboration for WhatsApp-based customer support.
GetChat.summary_abuse: WhatsApp business tool with standard data handling. GDPR-conscious design. No notable abuse patterns.

GetCommerce.history: GetCommerce is a Brazilian e-commerce platform that provides online store creation and management tools for the Brazilian market. Offers templates, payment integrations, and inventory management.
GetCommerce.summary_abuse: Brazilian e-commerce SaaS with standard practices. Serves local market with regional payment integrations. No notable abuse patterns.

GetFeedback.history: GetFeedback is a customer experience and survey platform originally founded in 2013, acquired by Momentive (formerly SurveyMonkey) in 2019. Provides CX surveys for Salesforce users with NPS, CSAT, and CES measurement.
GetFeedback.summary_abuse: B2B CX survey platform integrated with Salesforce. Standard data collection for survey responses. Part of the Momentive/SurveyMonkey ecosystem. No dark patterns.

Getgabs.history: Getgabs is a WhatsApp Business API platform and CRM that helps businesses manage WhatsApp communications, run marketing campaigns, and provide customer support through WhatsApp.
Getgabs.summary_abuse: WhatsApp business platform with standard messaging data handling. No notable dark patterns. Serves Indian market primarily.

Getintent.history: Getintent is a programmatic advertising and data management platform (DMP) providing audience targeting, ad serving, and analytics. Originally from Russia, it operates in the digital advertising space.
Getintent.summary_abuse: Programmatic advertising platform with higher tracker density as core to business model. Handles user data for ad targeting. Standard industry practices for ad tech.

GetMeAShop.history: GetMeAShop is an Indian e-commerce platform launched around 2012 that helps merchants create online stores. Focused on the Indian market with local payment gateway integration and vernacular language support.
GetMeAShop.summary_abuse: Indian e-commerce SaaS platform with standard practices. Serves SMB merchants in India. No notable abuse patterns.

GetMoreStudents.history: GetMoreStudents is a marketing and lead generation platform designed specifically for educational institutions and tutoring businesses. Helps schools and tutors attract new students through digital marketing.
GetMoreStudents.summary_abuse: Niche marketing SaaS for education sector. Standard lead generation practices. No notable dark patterns.

GetResponse.history: GetResponse is a comprehensive email marketing and automation platform founded in 1998 in Poland by Simon Grabowski. One of the oldest email marketing platforms, it has expanded to include landing pages, webinars, and e-commerce marketing features. Serves 350,000+ customers worldwide.
GetResponse.summary_abuse: Established email marketing platform with standard practices. Some aggressive upsell in interface but within normal SaaS bounds. GDPR compliant as EU company. Transparent pricing.

GetReview.history: GetReview is a review collection and management platform that helps businesses gather customer reviews and display them on their websites. Provides widget integration and review management tools.
GetReview.summary_abuse: Review collection SaaS with standard practices. No notable abuse patterns. Straightforward B2B tool.

Getro.history: Getro is a talent network platform for venture capital firms and their portfolio companies. Helps VC-backed companies share talent, post jobs, and build community networks. Founded around 2016.
Getro.summary_abuse: B2B talent platform for VC ecosystem. Standard professional data handling. No dark patterns. Transparent subscription pricing.

GetRooster.history: GetRooster is an employee onboarding and talent management platform that automates new hire processes. Provides tools for preboarding, task management, and employee experience.
GetRooster.summary_abuse: HR onboarding SaaS with standard data handling. Processes employee data. No notable dark patterns.

GetSimple CMS.history: GetSimple CMS is a lightweight flat-file content management system built on PHP and XML, created by Chris Cagle around 2009. Focuses on simplicity without a database requirement.
GetSimple CMS.summary_abuse: Abandoned open-source CMS project. No known abuse patterns. Development appears to have ceased around 2015-2016.

Getsitecontrol.history: Getsitecontrol is a SaaS popup and form builder that evolved into a combined on-site engagement and email marketing platform. Originally focused on website widget tools like surveys and contact forms, the platform expanded to include email broadcasts and automation. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Getsitecontrol.summary_abuse: Getsitecontrol is a popup and form builder used primarily for lead capture and email marketing. No significant documented abuse patterns are associated with the platform itself. As with any embeddable widget service, it can theoretically be used on phishing pages, but abuse is not a primary concern given its SMB and personal website audience and limited hosting capabilities.

GetSocial.history: GetSocial is a social engagement platform for mobile apps, providing referral campaigns, social sharing, and analytics. Founded around 2013, helps game and app developers implement viral growth mechanics.
GetSocial.summary_abuse: Mobile app social engagement platform. Collects user interaction and sharing data. Privacy practices focused on mobile analytics. No dark patterns.

GetTerms.history: GetTerms is a legal policy generator that creates Privacy Policies, Terms of Service, and other legal documents for websites and apps. Provides templates and customization tools.
GetTerms.summary_abuse: Legal document generator SaaS. Ironic that it helps with privacy policies. No dark patterns. Straightforward tool with standard subscription.

getUBetter.history: getUBetter is a UK-based digital healthcare platform providing online mental health and physiotherapy support services. Limited founding details are publicly available.
getUBetter.summary_abuse: getUBetter is a healthcare platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves NHS and private healthcare providers and does not expose content-hosting or anonymous sign-up features that fraudsters would target.

GetYourGuide.history: GetYourGuide is a travel experiences booking platform founded in 2009 in Berlin, Germany. Allows travelers to book tours, activities, and attractions worldwide. Major player in the online experiences market with over 100,000 activities listed.
GetYourGuide.summary_abuse: Travel booking platform with standard e-commerce practices. Cancellation policies are generally clear. GDPR compliant. Some aggressive email marketing after booking.

Gevme.history: Gevme is an event management and virtual events platform founded in 2008 in Singapore. Provides tools for event registration, attendee management, virtual events, and marketing for conferences and exhibitions.
Gevme.summary_abuse: Event management SaaS with standard data practices for attendee management. No notable abuse patterns. APAC-focused company with GDPR awareness.

Ghost.history: Ghost was created by John O'Nolan, a former WordPress core contributor, following a 2012 blog post that generated overwhelming interest. He and co-founder Hannah Wolfe ran a successful Kickstarter campaign in April 2013 to build a publishing-focused CMS. The first public release came in October 2013. Ghost is operated by a non-profit foundation, ensuring it remains independent of shareholders. It is built on Node.js and has grown into one of the leading open-source publishing platforms for newsletters and independent media.
Ghost.summary_abuse: Ghost is a widely used open-source publishing platform with a strong legitimate user base. As a self-hostable platform it can be installed on any server, which means some Ghost instances may be used for spammy content sites or low-quality publishing farms. However, abuse is a marginal share of total use, and the Ghost Foundation has a responsible disclosure policy and AUP for its hosted Ghost(Pro) service.

Gigalixir.history: Gigalixir is a cloud hosting platform specifically designed for Elixir and Phoenix applications. Founded in 2017, provides zero-downtime deployments, clustering, and managed PostgreSQL for Elixir developers.
Gigalixir.summary_abuse: Niche developer platform focused on Elixir hosting. Standard PaaS data practices. No dark patterns. Developer-focused with technical transparency.

Gijgo.history: Gijgo is an open-source collection of jQuery widgets including grids, date pickers, trees, and other UI components. Created around 2015, provides responsive web controls built on top of jQuery.
Gijgo.summary_abuse: Open-source jQuery widget library with no commercial interests. No abuse patterns. Free library with MIT license.

Giosg.history: Giosg was founded in 2011 in Helsinki, Finland by CEO Ville Rissanen. The platform started as a live chat and customer engagement tool for businesses. In April 2023 Giosg acquired Whisbi, a Spanish live shopping SaaS provider, expanding its capabilities in video-based sales and live commerce. The company has grown to serve over 1,200 customers worldwide.
Giosg.summary_abuse: Giosg is a B2B customer engagement platform used by enterprise customers in retail, automotive, and financial services. No documented abuse patterns are associated with the platform. Its enterprise sales model and targeted industry focus result in very limited exposure to consumer-facing fraud or spam.

Giscus.history: Giscus is an open-source comment system powered by GitHub Discussions. Created by Adhitya Mahen in 2021, it allows websites to use GitHub Discussions as a comment backend, popular with static site generators.
Giscus.summary_abuse: Open-source comment system with GitHub integration. No abuse patterns. Privacy-respecting alternative to Disqus. Requires GitHub account for comments.

Gist.history: Gist is a customer communication and marketing platform offering email marketing, live chat, knowledge base, and CRM features. Founded around 2017, positions itself as an all-in-one HubSpot alternative.
Gist.summary_abuse: All-in-one customer platform with standard SaaS practices. Moderate tracker use for its own analytics. No notable dark patterns.

Gist Giftship.history: Gist Giftship is a Shopify app that enables gift-related features for e-commerce stores including gift notes, wrapping options, and scheduled delivery. Part of the Gist e-commerce tool ecosystem.
Gist Giftship.summary_abuse: Simple Shopify gifting functionality app. No dark patterns. Standard subscription with clear Shopify app store pricing.

Gista.history: Gista is a customer support and engagement platform that integrates WhatsApp and other messaging channels for e-commerce businesses. Provides shared inbox and automation for customer communications.
Gista.summary_abuse: Customer messaging platform with standard data handling. No notable dark patterns. Serves e-commerce merchants.

git.history: Git was created by Linus Torvalds in 2005 to manage Linux kernel development after a dispute with the BitKeeper VCS vendor. It rapidly became the world's dominant distributed version control system and is maintained by the Git community with Junio C Hamano as the primary maintainer since 2005.
git.summary_abuse: Git is a foundational distributed version control system with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal; it is a backend developer tool that is not a target for or enabler of fraud.

GitBook.history: GitBook is a documentation and knowledge management platform founded in 2014 in Lyon, France. Allows teams to create, organize, and publish documentation with a git-based workflow. Used by many developer-focused companies.
GitBook.summary_abuse: Developer documentation platform with clean privacy practices. Standard SaaS with freemium model. Some email engagement but no dark patterns.

Gitea.history: Gitea is a community-managed fork of Gogs, a lightweight self-hosted git service written in Go. Started in 2016 by volunteers to provide a more actively maintained alternative to Gogs. Now backed by Gitea Ltd as a hosted service.
Gitea.summary_abuse: Lightweight open-source git hosting with strong community. No abuse patterns. Privacy-respecting self-hosted option. MIT licensed.

GitHub Pages.history: GitHub Pages is a free static site hosting service provided by GitHub (owned by Microsoft). Launched in 2008, it allows GitHub users to host websites directly from GitHub repositories. Widely used by developers and open-source projects.
GitHub Pages.summary_abuse: Free static hosting from Microsoft-owned GitHub. Privacy governed by GitHub/Microsoft privacy policy. No dark patterns. Simple and transparent service.

Gitiles.history: Gitiles is a simple repository browser for Git repositories built by Google. It powers the git.kernel.org and android.googlesource.com repositories. Read-only web interface for browsing git repos.
Gitiles.summary_abuse: Simple open-source git repository browser from Google. No abuse patterns. Used for public git hosting at major open-source projects.

GitLab.history: GitLab is a comprehensive DevOps platform founded in 2011 by Dmitriy Zaporozhets and Sid Sijbrandij in the Netherlands. Started as an open-source git repository manager and expanded to include CI/CD, container registry, issue tracking, and security scanning. IPO'd in October 2021.
GitLab.summary_abuse: Major open-source DevOps platform with strong security culture. Security disclosures program is active. FOSS core with enterprise paid features. No dark patterns.

GitLab CI/CD.history: GitLab CI/CD is the continuous integration and deployment component of GitLab, introduced in GitLab 8.0 (2015). It uses YAML-based pipeline configuration and runs on shared or self-hosted GitLab Runners. Became a leading CI/CD solution.
GitLab CI/CD.summary_abuse: Part of the GitLab open-source DevOps platform. No abuse patterns. Industry-standard CI/CD tooling with strong security posture.

gitlist.history: GitList is an open-source PHP-based web interface for browsing Git repositories, created by Fernando Vieira around 2011; it is an alternative to gitweb and cgit focused on a cleaner UI.
gitlist.summary_abuse: GitList is a self-hosted git repository browser with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

Gitter.history: Gitter is a developer community chat platform originally launched in 2013 and acquired by GitLab in 2017. Provided chat rooms tied to GitHub repositories. GitLab bridged Gitter with Matrix and the service was eventually shut down in 2023.
Gitter.summary_abuse: Discontinued developer chat service. Was absorbed into the Matrix ecosystem. No current active abuse concerns.

gitweb.history: gitweb is the built-in web interface for Git repositories, distributed as part of the Git project itself since 2005; it is a CGI-based Perl script providing basic repository browsing, predating and now largely superseded by more feature-rich alternatives like cgit and GitList.
gitweb.summary_abuse: gitweb is a self-hosted git repository browser bundled with Git itself; it has no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

GiveCampus.history: GiveCampus is a digital fundraising platform built specifically for educational institutions including colleges, universities, and K-12 schools. Founded in 2015 in Washington DC, it provides crowdfunding campaigns, peer-to-peer fundraising, and giving day tools.
GiveCampus.summary_abuse: Fundraising SaaS for educational institutions. Handles donor payment data with PCI compliance. No dark patterns. Standard nonprofit-focused fundraising platform.

Giveffect.history: Giveffect was founded around 2012 as a nonprofit CRM and fundraising platform headquartered in the United States. It targets nonprofit organizations with an integrated suite covering donor management, volunteer tracking, event management, and fundraising. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Giveffect.summary_abuse: Giveffect is a nonprofit CRM with no notable abuse signal. It operates as a closed SaaS with nonprofit customers, limiting exposure to consumer-facing attack surfaces. No public incidents or scam associations documented.

GiveSmart.history: GiveSmart is a fundraising and event management platform for nonprofits, providing auction management, text-to-give, and online fundraising tools. Owned by Community Brands, a nonprofit software company.
GiveSmart.summary_abuse: Nonprofit fundraising platform with standard security for payment processing. Part of Community Brands family. PCI compliant for donation processing.

GiveWP.history: GiveWP is the leading WordPress donation plugin, originally launched in 2014 by Matt Cromwell and Devin Walker as a dedicated fundraising plugin. It became one of the most popular nonprofit tools for WordPress, now maintained by StellarWP (Liquid Web company).
GiveWP.summary_abuse: Open-source WordPress donation plugin from a reputable company. No abuse patterns. Clear freemium model with transparent add-on pricing. PCI compliant payment handling.

GivingFuel.history: GivingFuel is a US-based nonprofit fundraising platform that has been operating for over 15 years, serving churches and nonprofit organizations with online giving tools. The platform offers donation pages, donor CRM, and marketing tools. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
GivingFuel.summary_abuse: GivingFuel is a nonprofit donation platform primarily serving churches and charitable organizations. Donation platforms can be exploited by fraudulent organizations or fake charities to collect funds, though GivingFuel's focus on legitimate nonprofits limits this exposure. No specific documented abuse incidents are known for this platform.

Gladly.history: Gladly was founded in 2014 in San Francisco by Michael Wolfe, Joseph Ansanelli, and Dirk Kessler. The company built an AI-powered customer service platform focused on people-centric support, treating customers as individuals rather than ticket numbers. Gladly raised over $208M across six funding rounds, including a $55M round in 2022 led by Riverwood Capital and a $40M round in 2024 led by AXA Venture Partners. Revenue reached $47.7M in 2024.
Gladly.summary_abuse: Gladly is an enterprise customer service platform used by retail and consumer brands. Its B2B nature and enterprise-only pricing model mean it has minimal exposure to consumer-facing abuse or fraud. No documented abuse incidents are associated with the platform.

Glami.history: Glami is a fashion product aggregator and price comparison platform founded in 2011 in the Czech Republic. It expanded across Central and Eastern Europe. In 2019, Heureka Group became a major stakeholder. Glami operates in over 10 countries.
Glami.summary_abuse: Glami is a fashion aggregator with no notable abuse signal. It aggregates listings from partner retailers and does not allow arbitrary user-generated content.

GlampManager.history: GlampManager is a property management software focused on glamping and outdoor hospitality businesses, founded around 2015 in the US. No major ownership changes documented.
GlampManager.summary_abuse: GlampManager is a niche hospitality management SaaS for glamping operators with no documented abuse signal.

Glance.history: Glance (glance.cx) is a co-browsing and visual engagement platform for customer support founded around 2019, headquartered in the US. No major ownership changes documented.
Glance.summary_abuse: Glance is a co-browsing SaaS used for customer support with no documented abuse signal. It operates in a B2B context with credentialed access.

Glassbox.history: Glassbox was founded in 2010 in Israel by Yoav Schreiber, Yaron Gueta, and Hanan Blumstein. The company provides digital experience analytics and session replay for enterprise regulated industries. In June 2021 Glassbox raised $100M through an IPO on the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange at a valuation near $500M.
Glassbox.summary_abuse: Glassbox captures session replays and user interaction data across enterprise web and mobile applications. Its session recording capabilities have raised privacy questions in regulated industries, particularly regarding the capture of form fields and sensitive user data. The company serves enterprises in financial services and insurance, and positions itself around compliance and privacy controls. No specific security incidents are publicly documented.

GlassFish.history: GlassFish is a Java EE application server originally developed by Sun Microsystems starting in 2006. Oracle acquired Sun in 2010. In 2017 Oracle dropped commercial support for GlassFish, transitioning it to the Eclipse Foundation as Eclipse GlassFish. It remains the reference implementation of Jakarta EE.
GlassFish.summary_abuse: GlassFish is a Java EE application server with no notable abuse signal. Abuse occurs through applications deployed on it. Historical CVEs have been patched. No specific abuse pattern is associated with GlassFish deployments.

Glassix.history: Founded in 2015 in Tel Aviv, Israel by Guy Shalom and Yoad Rashty as an omnichannel messaging platform for service and sales contact centers; no notable acquisitions since.
Glassix.summary_abuse: Glassix is a B2B omnichannel messaging platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves contact centers and does not expose consumer-facing anonymous content hosting.

Gleam.history: Gleam was founded on April 1, 2013 by Stuart McKeown and is based in Australia. The platform helps businesses run viral giveaways, contests, and growth campaigns with over 1 million businesses served worldwide. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Gleam.summary_abuse: Gleam is a viral marketing and giveaway platform. These platforms are frequently abused to run fake giveaway scams or artificially inflate social media metrics. Gleam requires social actions like follows and shares as contest entries, which can be used to manufacture fake engagement. The platform has an AUP but enforcement history is unclear. Fake or misleading giveaways are a known abuse vector for this category of tool.

Gleap.history: Founded in 2020 in Dornbirn, Austria as an all-in-one customer feedback and bug reporting platform; no notable ownership changes since.
Gleap.summary_abuse: Gleap is a customer feedback and bug reporting SaaS with no notable abuse signal. It serves software teams and does not offer public content hosting or anonymous signups.

Gleen.history: Gleen is an AI-powered customer support chatbot platform founded around 2022 in the US. Founded by former Nutanix employees.
Gleen.summary_abuse: Gleen is a B2B AI chatbot SaaS with no notable abuse signal. Theoretical prompt injection risk but no public incidents.

Glia.history: Glia is a digital customer service platform founded in 2012 in the US. Previously known as SaleMove, it rebranded to Glia in 2019. It focuses on financial services.
Glia.summary_abuse: Glia is an enterprise customer service platform targeting financial institutions with no notable abuse signal.

Glide.js.history: Glide.js is a JavaScript ES6 slider and carousel component created by Jedrzej Chalubek, first released in 2016. It is lightweight and dependency-free under MIT license.
Glide.js.summary_abuse: Glide.js is a frontend carousel library with no abuse surface. It is a pure client-side UI component with no network calls or data collection.

Glider.js.history: Glider.js is a lightweight responsive carousel library created by Nick Piscitelli, released around 2018. It is dependency-free and focused on smooth scrolling performance.
Glider.js.summary_abuse: Glider.js is a pure frontend carousel library with no abuse surface.

Glitch.history: Glitch originated from Fog Creek Software, founded in 2000 by Joel Spolsky and Michael Pryor. The web coding platform launched in spring 2017 as HyperDev, and was renamed Glitch in September 2018 when the company rebranded from Fog Creek. Glitch raised $30M in 2019. In May 2022 Fastly acquired Glitch for $40M. On July 8, 2025 Glitch discontinued all hosting services, citing high costs and platform abuse as primary reasons.
Glitch.summary_abuse: Glitch became well-known for abuse due to its free hosting with custom subdomains and minimal KYC requirements. Security researchers documented its use for hosting phishing pages, malware command-and-control infrastructure, and bot code. The platform's cited reason for shutdown in July 2025 explicitly included platform abuse, underscoring that abuse was a significant share of activity. Fastly's acquisition and eventual shutdown represent a vendor response to chronic anti-abuse challenges.

GlitchTip.history: GlitchTip is an open-source error tracking platform created around 2019 as a self-hostable alternative to Sentry. It supports Sentry-compatible SDKs and is written in Python/Django.
GlitchTip.summary_abuse: GlitchTip is a self-hosted error monitoring tool with no notable abuse signal. It is backend infrastructure with no consumer-facing surface.

Global-e.history: Global-e is a cross-border e-commerce enablement platform founded in 2013 in Israel. It listed on the Nasdaq in 2021 (GLBE). Shopify acquired a significant stake as part of a strategic partnership in 2021.
Global-e.summary_abuse: Global-e handles cross-border payments with standard card-not-present fraud exposure but operates under regulated payment oversight with PCI-DSS compliance. No notable platform-specific abuse incidents.

GlobalShopex.history: GlobalShopex is a cross-border e-commerce and international shipping solution founded around 2008 in the US. No major acquisitions documented.
GlobalShopex.summary_abuse: GlobalShopex is a cross-border shipping provider with standard e-commerce fraud exposure. No specific notable incidents documented.

Globo Also Bought.history: Globo Also Bought is a Shopify app developed by Globo Software Solutions, a Vietnamese company, adding frequently-bought-together product recommendations to Shopify stores.
Globo Also Bought.summary_abuse: Globo Also Bought is a Shopify app for product recommendations with no documented abuse signal.

Globo Color Swatch.history: Globo Color Swatch is a Shopify app by Globo (a Vietnamese app developer) that adds color and image swatches for product variants on Shopify stores; no notable ownership changes documented.
Globo Color Swatch.summary_abuse: Globo Color Swatch is a Shopify product variant display app with no notable abuse signal. It operates within the Shopify merchant ecosystem and has no consumer-facing content hosting surface.

Globo Form Builder.history: Globo Form Builder is a Shopify contact form builder app by Globo (a Vietnamese Shopify app developer); no notable ownership changes documented.
Globo Form Builder.summary_abuse: Globo Form Builder is a Shopify contact form app with no notable abuse signal. It operates within the Shopify merchant ecosystem and collects form submissions for store operators only.

Globo Pre-Order.history: Globo Pre-Order is a Shopify pre-order management app by Globo (a Vietnamese Shopify app developer); no notable ownership changes documented.
Globo Pre-Order.summary_abuse: Globo Pre-Order is a Shopify pre-order functionality app with no notable abuse signal. It operates within the Shopify merchant ecosystem and manages pre-order flows for store operators.

GlobRes.history: GlobRes is a global hotel reservation system founded around 2000 providing central reservation software for independent hotels.
GlobRes.summary_abuse: GlobRes is a hotel reservation SaaS with no documented abuse signal. It operates in B2B hospitality context.

Glofox.history: Glofox is a gym and fitness studio management software founded in 2014 in Dublin, Ireland. It raised Series B funding and was acquired by ABC Fitness Solutions in 2022.
Glofox.summary_abuse: Glofox is a fitness studio management SaaS with no notable abuse signal. It operates in B2B fitness industry context.

Gloo.history: Gloo is an open-source API gateway and service mesh built on Envoy Proxy, developed by Solo.io, founded in 2017. Solo.io raised Series C funding. Gloo Edge is the ingress controller variant.
Gloo.summary_abuse: Gloo is an API gateway with no notable abuse signal. It is backend infrastructure deployed in private cloud environments.

Glopal.history: Glopal was founded in 2020 by Patrick Smarzynski and Benjamin Cohen, who previously co-founded Webinterpret in 2007. The company spun out of Webinterpret as a sister company to address cross-border ecommerce for large brands and retailers. Glopal raised 20M euros led by Hi Inov Dentressangle and Credit Mutuel Innovation. The platform is headquartered in Paris with offices in eight countries.
Glopal.summary_abuse: Glopal is a B2B cross-border ecommerce localization platform serving enterprise retailers and luxury brands. Its enterprise-only model and narrow use case mean minimal exposure to consumer-facing abuse or fraud. No documented abuse patterns are associated with the platform.

GloriaFood.history: GloriaFood is an online food ordering system for restaurants, founded in 2012 in Romania. It offers a free tier for small restaurants. Acquired by Oracle in 2021 and rebranded as Oracle MICROS Online Ordering.
GloriaFood.summary_abuse: GloriaFood is a restaurant ordering platform with minor abuse exposure typical of any online ordering system. No significant specific abuse incidents documented.

Glow.history: Glow is a customer loyalty and rewards platform for ecommerce businesses, helping merchants increase repeat sales through structured loyalty programs. The platform integrates with Shopify and other ecommerce platforms. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Glow.summary_abuse: Glow is an ecommerce loyalty program platform with no notable documented abuse patterns. Its use case of rewarding repeat customers on established ecommerce stores provides minimal surface area for fraud or misuse.

GLPI.history: GLPI (Gestionnaire Libre de Parc Informatique) is a free and open-source IT asset management and service desk software first released in 2003 by Teclib. It is widely used in France and internationally, particularly in public sector organizations and enterprises. Teclib founded as the commercial entity behind GLPI, offering cloud and support services. GLPI has grown to be one of the most popular IT service management tools in French-speaking countries.
GLPI.summary_abuse: GLPI is an open-source IT service management and asset tracking tool. As enterprise IT infrastructure software with no consumer-facing surface, it presents minimal abuse risk. CVEs have been found in GLPI over the years, some allowing authentication bypass or remote code execution, but these are software security issues rather than platform-level abuse.

GLS.history: GLS (General Logistics Systems) was founded in 1989 in the Netherlands. It grew into a major European parcel delivery network, operating across 41 countries. Royal Mail Group acquired GLS in 2000 and it remains a wholly-owned subsidiary. GLS operates as an independent network with its own IT systems for shipment tracking and e-commerce integration.
GLS.summary_abuse: GLS is a major European parcel delivery company. Its tracking and logistics APIs are enterprise B2B tools. The GLS brand is commonly spoofed in parcel delivery phishing campaigns targeting consumers, but this is an external brand abuse issue rather than a platform vulnerability. GLS itself has standard anti-phishing measures in place.

GlueUp.history: Founded in 2013 and headquartered in McLean, Virginia, USA; Glue Up (formerly EventBank) is an all-in-one CRM and event management platform serving associations and nonprofits globally; no notable acquisitions.
GlueUp.summary_abuse: GlueUp is a CRM and event management platform for associations with no notable abuse signal. It does not offer consumer-facing content hosting or anonymous signups.

Glyphicons.history: Glyphicons is an icon font library created by Jan Kovvár, first released around 2010. It gained widespread adoption as the default icon set bundled with Bootstrap 2 and Bootstrap 3. Bootstrap 4 dropped the bundled Glyphicons dependency.
Glyphicons.summary_abuse: Glyphicons is a static icon font library with no abuse surface.

Gnuboard.history: Gnuboard is an open-source bulletin board and CMS system popular in South Korea, created around 2000. It is widely used for Korean-language websites and community portals.
Gnuboard.summary_abuse: Gnuboard is a CMS popular in South Korea. As with any CMS that allows user-generated content, spam and unwanted posts are common operational concerns, but the platform itself has no outsized abuse profile.

Go.history: Go (also called Golang) is a statically typed compiled language created by Robert Griesemer, Rob Pike, and Ken Thompson at Google and publicly released in 2009. It was designed to address perceived shortcomings in other languages at Google. Go is open source and maintained by Google.
Go.summary_abuse: Go is a foundational programming language with no consumer-facing abuse surface.

Go Instore.history: Founded in 2014 in the UK as a live video retail platform connecting online shoppers with in-store experts; acquired by Emplifi in September 2021 and integrated as their live commerce product.
Go Instore.summary_abuse: Go Instore is a B2B live video commerce platform with no notable abuse signal. It connects online customers with retail staff via live video and does not offer anonymous content hosting.

GoAffPro.history: GoAffPro is an affiliate marketing platform for ecommerce stores, integrating with Shopify, WooCommerce, and BigCommerce. It was founded around 2017 and offers both a free tier and premium plans for small businesses. The platform allows merchants to set up branded affiliate portals where customers and influencers can register, generate referral links, and earn commissions on sales. It is primarily popular in the Shopify app ecosystem.
GoAffPro.summary_abuse: GoAffPro is an affiliate marketing platform with moderate abuse potential. Affiliate programs in general are susceptible to affiliate fraud (fake clicks, cookie stuffing, or self-referrals), and small platforms like GoAffPro have limited automated fraud detection compared to larger enterprise solutions. No major documented abuse incidents specific to GoAffPro have been publicly reported.

GoAhead.history: GoAhead is a lightweight embedded web server originally developed by GoAhead Software in 1995. EmbedThis Software acquired the codebase and continues to maintain the open-source version. Used widely in embedded devices and IoT.
GoAhead.summary_abuse: GoAhead is an embedded web server with no notable consumer-facing abuse surface.

GoAnywhere.history: GoAnywhere MFT is a managed file transfer platform developed by Fortra (formerly HelpSystems), first released around 2003. In early 2023, a critical zero-day vulnerability (CVE-2023-0669) was exploited by the Clop ransomware gang to attack over 130 organizations before a patch was released.
GoAnywhere.summary_abuse: GoAnywhere MFT was exploited in a major 2023 ransomware campaign (Clop/CVE-2023-0669) affecting over 130 organizations including healthcare and financial firms. This was a supply-chain-style attack on the file transfer platform itself. Fortra issued patches promptly but the zero-day window caused significant damage.

Goat Slider.history: Goat Slider is a Webflow JavaScript slider library; limited public history available.
Goat Slider.summary_abuse: Goat Slider is a Webflow slider library with no notable abuse signal. It is a client-side UI component with no hosted surface.

Goat Systems.history: Goat Systems is a small marketing automation and CRM software vendor; limited public history available.
Goat Systems.summary_abuse: Goat Systems is a small niche marketing automation CRM with no notable abuse signal documented.

GoatCounter.history: GoatCounter was created by Martin Tournoij (arp242) and released in 2019 as a privacy-friendly alternative to Google Analytics. Written in Go, it does not use cookies or track individual users, positioning itself as GDPR-compliant by design. The project is donation-supported and maintained by a single developer, with an active community on GitHub. It is available as a hosted service and as a self-hosted open-source binary.
GoatCounter.summary_abuse: GoatCounter has no meaningful documented abuse exposure. It is a privacy-focused analytics tool that does not collect personally identifiable information, making it an unlikely target for data theft or misuse. As an open-source project maintained by a single developer, it lacks dedicated anti-abuse infrastructure but the tool's minimal data collection scope limits risk.

GOb2b.history: GOb2b is a Portuguese B2B e-commerce platform for wholesale and distributor orders, founded around 2010. It allows companies to create a branded online ordering portal for their business customers. No notable acquisitions or controversial events are documented.
GOb2b.summary_abuse: GOb2b is a Portuguese B2B e-commerce platform with no documented abuse incidents. As a business-to-business ordering tool, its consumer-facing surface is limited and abuse risk is low.

Gobot.history: Founded in 2017 and headquartered in New Jersey, USA; Gobot is a conversational chatbot platform for boosting website leads and sales; no notable acquisitions.
Gobot.summary_abuse: Gobot is a website chatbot and lead generation platform with no notable abuse signal. It does not offer public content hosting or anonymous signups.

GoCache.history: GoCache was founded in 2013 in Brazil as a CDN and WAF platform focused on the Brazilian market. It provides content delivery, DDoS protection, and Web Application Firewall services, with points of presence across all regions of Brazil. The platform protects over 150,000 websites and applications. No major ownership changes or acquisitions are documented.
GoCache.summary_abuse: GoCache is a CDN and WAF infrastructure provider primarily serving the Brazilian market. CDN services can be used to cache and deliver malicious content, though GoCache's WAF positioning and active DDoS protection suggest a security-aware posture. No specific public abuse incidents are documented for this provider.

GoCertify.history: GoCertify is a UK-based bootstrapped verification platform founded by Jonny Matthews; it verifies eligibility for student, key worker, military, and age-based discounts for retailers; grown to 350+ retail partners without external investment.
GoCertify.summary_abuse: GoCertify is an eligibility verification platform for retail discounts. The verification gateway model could potentially be probed for bypass attempts, but the company serves legitimate retailers and its primary use case is preventing fraudulent discount claims rather than enabling them.

GoDaddy.history: GoDaddy was founded in 1997 by Bob Parsons as Jomax Technologies, rebranded to GoDaddy in 1999. It became the world's largest domain registrar. GoDaddy went public on the NYSE in 2015. It has grown through numerous acquisitions in domains, hosting, and website building.
GoDaddy.summary_abuse: GoDaddy is heavily abused for domain registration in phishing and scam campaigns. As the world's largest domain registrar, a significant share of newly registered malicious domains use GoDaddy. The company processes abuse reports and suspends accounts but the sheer volume and low cost of domain registration makes it attractive to bad actors. GoDaddy has also suffered several major data breaches.

GoDaddy CoBlocks.history: GoDaddy CoBlocks is an open-source WordPress Gutenberg block plugin developed by GoDaddy, originally created by Rich Tabor in 2018 and later acquired by GoDaddy.
GoDaddy CoBlocks.summary_abuse: GoDaddy CoBlocks is a WordPress plugin with no notable abuse signal beyond standard WordPress plugin ecosystem considerations.

GoDaddy Domain Parking.history: GoDaddy Domain Parking is a service that places advertising pages on unused registered domains, part of GoDaddy's core domain business since the late 1990s.
GoDaddy Domain Parking.summary_abuse: GoDaddy Domain Parking is frequently associated with typosquatting and brand abuse. Parked domains with advertising often include malicious ads or misleading content. GoDaddy's scale as the world's largest registrar means a large volume of parked domains pass through its parking system.

GoDaddy Escapade.history: A GoDaddy-developed open-source WordPress child theme based on the Primer parent theme; hosted on GitHub under the godaddy-wordpress organization; no notable events beyond initial release.
GoDaddy Escapade.summary_abuse: This GoDaddy WordPress child theme has no notable abuse signal. It is a static theme file with no hosted surface for scammers to exploit.

GoDaddy Go.history: GoDaddy Go is an open-source Gutenberg-first WordPress theme developed and published by GoDaddy on GitHub; a standalone flexible theme not based on Primer; no notable events since release.
GoDaddy Go.summary_abuse: GoDaddy Go is an open-source WordPress theme with no notable abuse signal. It is a theme file without hosted services or consumer-facing attack surface.

GoDaddy Lyrical.history: A GoDaddy-developed open-source WordPress child theme based on the Primer parent theme; hosted on GitHub under the godaddy-wordpress organization; no notable events beyond initial release.
GoDaddy Lyrical.summary_abuse: This GoDaddy WordPress child theme has no notable abuse signal. It is a static theme file with no hosted surface for scammers to exploit.

GoDaddy Online Store.history: GoDaddy Online Store is an e-commerce builder included in GoDaddy's website builder products, evolved from various e-commerce acquisitions over the 2000s-2010s.
GoDaddy Online Store.summary_abuse: GoDaddy Online Store powers many small business storefronts. As a mainstream e-commerce platform, it has documented cases of fake stores and scam operations set up on its infrastructure. GoDaddy processes abuse reports but the ease of setup attracts fraudulent merchants.

GoDaddy Primer.history: GoDaddy Primer is an open-source WordPress parent theme developed by GoDaddy as the base for multiple child themes (Escapade, Lyrical, Uptown Style); hosted on GitHub; no notable events since initial release.
GoDaddy Primer.summary_abuse: GoDaddy Primer is an open-source WordPress parent theme with no notable abuse signal. It is a theme file without hosted services or consumer-facing attack surface.

GoDaddy Uptown Style.history: A GoDaddy-developed open-source WordPress child theme based on the Primer parent theme; hosted on GitHub under the godaddy-wordpress organization; no notable events beyond initial release.
GoDaddy Uptown Style.summary_abuse: This GoDaddy WordPress child theme has no notable abuse signal. It is a static theme file with no hosted surface for scammers to exploit.

GoDaddy Website Builder.history: GoDaddy Website Builder is a drag-and-drop website creation tool developed by GoDaddy, evolved from multiple platform acquisitions over the 2000s-2010s. Now powered by a proprietary builder.
GoDaddy Website Builder.summary_abuse: GoDaddy Website Builder is used to create fraudulent and scam websites. Its ease of use and GoDaddy's name recognition attract both legitimate users and bad actors. GoDaddy has published abuse policies and responds to takedown requests, but the scale of their hosting means abuse volumes are significant.

Godot.history: Godot is an open-source cross-platform game engine created by Juan Linietsky and Ariel Manzur. First released in 2014 after being open-sourced. It is now one of the most popular open-source game engines, managed by the Software Freedom Conservancy. Gained massive community growth after Unity's controversial pricing changes in 2023.
Godot.summary_abuse: Godot is a game engine with no notable abuse signal. It is a desktop development tool with no consumer-facing hosted surface.

Goftino.history: Goftino is an Iranian live chat service headquartered in Tehran, Iran; serves primarily Iranian websites; no notable ownership changes documented.
Goftino.summary_abuse: Goftino is an Iranian live chat platform primarily serving the Iranian market with no major documented abuse signal outside its region. The chat widget embeds on websites but does not offer arbitrary content hosting.

Gogs.history: Gogs is a self-hosted Git service written in Go, created by Unknwon (Jiahua Chen) and first released in 2014. Gitea was forked from Gogs in 2016 due to slow upstream development pace.
Gogs.summary_abuse: Gogs is a self-hosted Git server with no notable inherent abuse signal. Like any code hosting platform, individual instances could host malicious code, but abuse depends on deployment configuration.

GoJS.history: GoJS was launched in 2012 by Northwoods Software Corporation, founded in 1994 in New Hampshire, USA; it is a commercial JavaScript diagramming library for building interactive flowcharts and org charts; no notable ownership changes since.
GoJS.summary_abuse: GoJS is a commercial JavaScript diagramming library with no notable abuse signal. It is a client-side rendering library with no hosted content surface.

GoKwik.history: GoKwik is an Indian e-commerce enablement platform founded in 2020, focusing on checkout optimization and returns management for Indian online retailers. Raised Series C funding.
GoKwik.summary_abuse: GoKwik is a B2B e-commerce checkout optimization SaaS with no notable abuse signal.

Golden Volunteer.history: Golden Volunteer is a volunteer management software platform for nonprofits founded around 2010 in the US.
Golden Volunteer.summary_abuse: Golden Volunteer is a niche nonprofit volunteer management SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

Gomag.history: Gomag is a Romanian e-commerce platform founded in 2012 offering hosted online store solutions for Romanian merchants.
Gomag.summary_abuse: Gomag is a regional e-commerce platform with standard e-commerce abuse exposure. No notable specific incidents documented.

GoMage.history: GoMage is a Magento and Adobe Commerce development agency and extension provider with over 10 years of experience. The company provides custom Magento builds, PWA development, and a catalog of Magento extensions including LightCheckout, Feed Pro, and SEO Booster. GoMage is a certified Adobe Commerce partner. No major ownership changes are documented.
GoMage.summary_abuse: GoMage provides Magento development services and extensions used on merchant ecommerce sites. As backend development tools and extensions installed on customer servers, these have no direct consumer-facing abuse surface. No documented abuse incidents are associated with GoMage products.

Gong.history: Gong is a revenue intelligence platform founded in 2015 in Israel, headquartered in San Francisco. It uses AI to analyze sales calls and customer interactions. Reached unicorn valuation in 2020 and raised over $580M in funding.
Gong.summary_abuse: Gong records and analyzes sales calls, which involves processing sensitive business communications. Privacy concerns exist around call recording practices and data retention. Operates in enterprise context with SOC 2 compliance and generally responds to security reports.

Goober.history: Goober is a lightweight CSS-in-JS library released around 2020, inspired by styled-components but with a much smaller bundle size.
Goober.summary_abuse: Goober is a CSS-in-JS library with no abuse surface.

GoodAPI.history: GoodAPI is a sustainability API platform that lets e-commerce merchants plant trees, remove ocean plastic, or restore ecosystems with every sale; limited public founding information.
GoodAPI.summary_abuse: GoodAPI is a sustainability impact API with no notable abuse signal. It connects merchant transactions to environmental actions and does not expose consumer-facing content hosting.

GoodApps.history: GoodApps is an ecommerce tools builder providing customizable online store solutions; limited public history available.
GoodApps.summary_abuse: GoodApps is a small ecommerce tooling platform with no notable abuse signal documented.

Goodbits.history: Founded in 2014 as an email newsletter creation tool for curating and sending content newsletters; bootstrapped product; no notable ownership changes.
Goodbits.summary_abuse: Goodbits is a small newsletter creation tool with no notable abuse signal. It is used by legitimate publishers to curate email newsletters and does not offer arbitrary content hosting.

GoodEshop.history: GoodEshop is a Slovak online ecommerce management tool; limited public history available.
GoodEshop.summary_abuse: GoodEshop is a small regional ecommerce management tool with no notable abuse signal documented.

GoodReviews.history: GoodReviews is an online reputation management tool that displays and collects Google reviews for businesses; limited public history available.
GoodReviews.summary_abuse: GoodReviews is a Google review aggregation tool with no notable abuse signal. It displays public reviews and does not offer arbitrary content hosting or anonymous signups.

Goodsearch.history: Goodsearch is a search engine that donates a portion of ad revenue to nonprofits and charities, founded in 2005 in the US.
Goodsearch.summary_abuse: Goodsearch is a charitable search engine. As a search and advertising platform, it has exposure to ad fraud and low-quality ads typical of smaller ad networks.

Google Ads.history: Google Ads (formerly Google AdWords) was launched in 2000 by Google. It is the dominant online advertising platform globally, generating the majority of Alphabet's revenue. Rebranded from AdWords to Google Ads in 2018.
Google Ads.summary_abuse: Google Ads is widely abused for malvertising, phishing, and scam campaigns. Bad actors purchase Google Ads to appear at the top of search results for brand names and route victims to phishing pages. Google invests heavily in ad fraud detection but the scale and immediacy of ad serving makes it an attractive attack vector. Malvertising via Google Ads affects millions of users annually.

Google Ads Conversion Tracking.history: Google Ads Conversion Tracking is a feature of Google Ads launched around 2003 that tracks user actions after clicking ads. It embeds tracking pixels on advertisers' websites.
Google Ads Conversion Tracking.summary_abuse: Google Ads Conversion Tracking is a tracking pixel. As ad-tech infrastructure, it collects user behavioral data across the web. Its cross-site tracking capabilities contribute to fingerprinting and profiling, which regulators in the EU have scrutinized. No unique abuse vector beyond standard ad tracking.

Google AdSense.history: Google AdSense launched in March 2003 after Google acquired Applied Semantics for $102 million, whose contextual ad technology became the foundation of the product. AdSense became one of Google's most profitable products, accounting for 15% of revenues by 2005. The program evolved from text-only to display and rich media ads. In 2024, Google switched AdSense to an eCPM payment model replacing the old per-click model. Over 38 million websites use AdSense as of 2021.
Google AdSense.summary_abuse: Google AdSense has significant documented abuse. Invalid click fraud is a persistent problem where webmasters use bots or click farms to generate fraudulent ad revenue. Scraped content sites, auto-generated spam content, and made-for-AdSense (MFA) sites have historically exploited the program. Google employs automated invalid traffic detection and has banned publishers for policy violations. Malvertising through the AdSense network has also delivered malware to users via compromised or fraudulent ad creatives.

Google Analytics.history: Google Analytics launched in November 2005 after Google acquired Urchin Software Corporation in April 2005. It rapidly became the world's most widely deployed web analytics platform, reportedly used by over 55% of all websites. In 2012, Universal Analytics replaced the original version. In 2020, Google announced Google Analytics 4 as the successor, and in July 2023, the original Universal Analytics was officially deprecated in favor of GA4. GDPR and privacy law scrutiny has been significant, with several EU data protection authorities ruling that GA transfers data to the US in violation of GDPR.
Google Analytics.summary_abuse: Google Analytics itself is not directly abused in the traditional sense, but its JavaScript tag has been misused in supply chain attacks where adversaries inject modified GA tags to exfiltrate form data and credit card numbers from compromised websites. The platform also has a well-documented history of use in data theft via the Google Analytics Measurement Protocol, where attackers send stolen data as fake analytics events to avoid firewall detection. Google maintains active abuse monitoring and has a robust vulnerability disclosure program.

Google Analytics Enhanced eCommerce.history: Google Analytics Enhanced eCommerce is a feature extension of Google Analytics launched in 2014, adding detailed e-commerce tracking (product impressions, purchases, refunds) to standard GA reporting.
Google Analytics Enhanced eCommerce.summary_abuse: Google Analytics Enhanced eCommerce is an analytics feature that collects detailed purchase and product interaction data. Privacy concerns are similar to Google Analytics broadly, including data sharing with Google and cross-site tracking.

Google App Engine.history: Google App Engine (GAE) was launched in 2008 as Google's first public cloud service, allowing developers to build and host web applications. It was a pioneering PaaS offering.
Google App Engine.summary_abuse: Google App Engine is a cloud platform occasionally used to host phishing pages and malware distribution due to the legitimacy of the google.com/appspot.com domains. Google actively detects and removes abuse but the trusted domain remains attractive to attackers.

Google Call Conversion Tracking.history: Google Call Conversion Tracking is a feature within Google Ads (formerly Google AdWords) that tracks phone calls driven by search ads; part of Googles suite of conversion tracking tools which expanded over the 2010s as mobile search grew.
Google Call Conversion Tracking.summary_abuse: Google Call Conversion Tracking is a Google Ads feature. Legitimate use dominates overwhelmingly. Ad fraud exists across the broader Google Ads ecosystem, but Googles extensive anti-fraud systems and rapid response make the abuse profile comparable to other major ad platforms.

Google Charts.history: Google Charts is a free web charting service launched by Google in 2007; it renders interactive charts in the browser using JavaScript; Google deprecated the static image charts API in 2019 but the JavaScript interactive charts library remains active.
