Google Charts.summary_abuse: Google Charts is a free data visualization library with no notable abuse signal. It renders charts client-side and has no consumer-facing content hosting surface.

Google Cloud.history: Google Cloud Platform launched publicly in 2008 starting with App Engine, the first platform-as-a-service cloud offering. Google expanded it significantly through the 2010s, adding Compute Engine (IaaS), BigQuery, and Kubernetes (open-sourced in 2014). Google Cloud became a full-service cloud provider competing with AWS and Azure. By 2024, Google Cloud reported over $36 billion in annual revenue, becoming a major cloud market player with roughly 11% market share.
Google Cloud.summary_abuse: Google Cloud infrastructure is occasionally exploited for hosting malware, phishing sites, and C2 infrastructure due to Google's trusted domain reputation and flexible free-tier credits. Threat researchers have documented abuse of Google Cloud Storage and Cloud Run for phishing kit hosting and cryptomining. Google maintains active abuse detection systems and responds to reports, but the scale of the platform means abuse at the margins persists.

Google Cloud CDN.history: Google Cloud CDN is a content delivery network launched by Google in 2015 as part of Google Cloud Platform. It integrates with Google's global network infrastructure.
Google Cloud CDN.summary_abuse: Google Cloud CDN is used occasionally to serve malicious content due to the legitimacy of Google domains. Google actively monitors and removes policy-violating content but the trusted domain status is exploited by attackers.

Google Cloud Load Balancing.history: Google Cloud Load Balancing launched in 2013 as part of Google Cloud Platform, providing scalable global load balancing for applications on GCP.
Google Cloud Load Balancing.summary_abuse: Google Cloud Load Balancing is backend infrastructure with no notable direct abuse signal.

Google Cloud Storage.history: Google Cloud Storage launched in 2010 as a scalable object storage service for GCP. It competes with AWS S3 and Azure Blob Storage.
Google Cloud Storage.summary_abuse: Google Cloud Storage is occasionally abused for malware hosting and phishing kit distribution due to trusted storage.googleapis.com domains. Google enforces AUP and responds to abuse reports, but misconfigurations exposing public buckets have leaked sensitive data across many organizations.

Google Cloud Trace.history: Google Cloud Trace is a distributed tracing service for GCP applications, launched around 2015. It is part of Google's Cloud Operations suite.
Google Cloud Trace.summary_abuse: Google Cloud Trace is backend observability infrastructure with no notable abuse signal.

Google Code Prettify.history: Google Code Prettify is a JavaScript syntax highlighting library released around 2006, bundled with Google Code. It was widely used but officially deprecated in 2020 in favor of alternatives.
Google Code Prettify.summary_abuse: Google Code Prettify is a deprecated syntax highlighting library with no abuse surface.

Google Customer Reviews.history: Google Customer Reviews (formerly Google Trusted Stores) is a free review collection service by Google, launched around 2015, allowing e-commerce sites to collect seller ratings shown in Google Shopping and search.
Google Customer Reviews.summary_abuse: Google Customer Reviews can be subject to fake or manipulated reviews posted by competitors or bad actors. Google has review filtering systems but fake reviews remain an ongoing problem on its platforms.

Google Font API.history: Google Fonts is a free web font hosting service launched by Google in 2010, serving fonts via the fonts.googleapis.com CDN. It became one of the most widely used CDN services globally. In 2022 a German court found that embedding Google Fonts violated GDPR by transferring user IP addresses to Google without consent.
Google Font API.summary_abuse: Google Fonts faced GDPR enforcement in Germany in 2022 due to IP address transmission to Google servers. This led many European sites to self-host fonts. The fonts themselves have no malicious content but the CDN introduces a third-party dependency on Google infrastructure.

Google Forms.history: Google Forms launched in 2008 as part of Google Docs Suite, providing free online form and survey creation.
Google Forms.summary_abuse: Google Forms is heavily abused for phishing attacks. Attackers create fake forms collecting credentials, banking details, or personal information. The forms.google.com domain is trusted, making it effective for phishing. Google attempts to detect and remove abuse but the volume is high.

Google Hosted Libraries.history: Google Hosted Libraries is a CDN for popular JavaScript libraries, launched around 2007. It hosts libraries like jQuery, Bootstrap, and others via ajax.googleapis.com.
Google Hosted Libraries.summary_abuse: Google Hosted Libraries is a static CDN with no notable abuse signal. A concern is that CDN dependency creates a SPOF and allows Google to see which sites load which libraries.

Google Maps.history: Google Maps launched in February 2005 based on technology from Where 2 Technologies, which Google acquired in 2004. It introduced the AJAX-based interactive map paradigm. Google Earth was integrated in 2007. In 2012, Apple dropped Google Maps from iOS in favor of Apple Maps, reducing Google Maps to Android and web. Google Maps introduced a significant pricing change in 2018, converting to usage-based billing from the Google Maps Platform API, which upset many developers with sudden cost increases.
Google Maps.summary_abuse: Google Maps has low direct abuse risk as it is a mapping API. API keys for Google Maps are occasionally stolen and misused for unauthorized geolocation lookups, resulting in unexpected billing for legitimate customers. Google provides usage quotas and monitoring to detect key abuse. The platform does collect substantial location and usage data from users, which has drawn privacy scrutiny from regulators.

Google My Business.history: Google My Business (now Google Business Profile) launched in 2014, replacing Google Places and Google+ Local. It allows businesses to manage their Google Search and Maps presence.
Google My Business.summary_abuse: Google Business Profile is targeted by spam and fake listing abuse. Bad actors create fake business listings, hijack existing legitimate listings, or post fake reviews. This is a significant problem for local SEO and can redirect customers to fraudulent businesses.

Google Optimize.history: Google Optimize was an A/B testing and website optimization tool launched by Google in 2016. Google deprecated the service in September 2023.
Google Optimize.summary_abuse: Google Optimize was a testing and optimization tool that has been deprecated. No notable abuse signal.

Google PageSpeed.history: Google PageSpeed (PageSpeed Insights) is a web performance analysis tool launched by Google in 2009.
Google PageSpeed.summary_abuse: Google PageSpeed is a web performance analysis tool with no notable abuse signal.

Google Pay.history: Google Pay evolved from Android Pay (launched 2015) and Google Wallet (launched 2011), merging them into a single brand in 2018. The service enables contactless in-store payments, online checkout, and peer-to-peer transfers. Google Pay has faced regional inconsistencies, with a major US app redesign in 2020 being largely discontinued in 2023 when Google refocused the US app on the Google Wallet brand while Google Pay continued in other markets.
Google Pay.summary_abuse: Google Pay is targeted by social engineering scams where fraudsters impersonate Google Pay support to trick users into sending money, a tactic common across all P2P payment platforms. The platform enforces strong fraud detection and chargeback protections for merchant transactions. Direct exploitation of the Google Pay API is rare due to stringent PCI-DSS compliance requirements and tokenization architecture.

Google Publisher Tag.history: Google Publisher Tag (GPT) is the JavaScript ad tagging library for Google Ad Manager (formerly DoubleClick for Publishers). It replaced the older asynchronous tag format and has been the standard mechanism for serving Google display ads on publisher websites since the late 2000s. GPT enables publishers to define ad slots, targeting, and auction logic on their pages and is central to the Google advertising ecosystem.
Google Publisher Tag.summary_abuse: Google Publisher Tag is part of the ad-serving ecosystem and inherits risks common to ad tech: malvertising campaigns have used GPT-served ad slots to deliver malicious JavaScript or redirect users to scam pages. Google operates the DoubleClick/AdSense infrastructure that publishers integrate GPT with, which faces ongoing malvertising threats. Google has anti-malvertising systems but incidents occur at scale given the billions of ad impressions served daily.

Google Sign-in.history: Google Sign-in (OAuth/OIDC-based identity) launched around 2011 and became widely used for third-party app authentication. Part of Google Identity Services.
Google Sign-in.summary_abuse: Google Sign-in is frequently spoofed in phishing campaigns. Attackers create fake Google login pages to steal credentials. Additionally, open redirect vulnerabilities in OAuth implementations have been exploited. Google has strong anti-phishing infrastructure but is the most commonly impersonated brand in phishing.

Google Sites.history: Google Sites is a free website creation tool launched by Google in 2008, rebuilt in 2016 with a new interface.
Google Sites.summary_abuse: Google Sites is heavily abused for phishing and scam pages. The sites.google.com domain is trusted by email filters and browsers, making it popular for hosting credential-harvesting pages and fake business sites. Google detects and removes abuse but volume remains significant.

Google Tag Manager.history: Google Tag Manager launched in October 2012 to simplify the deployment of analytics and marketing tags on websites without requiring code changes. It replaced the need to hardcode tracking scripts and enabled non-developers to manage tag firing rules. GTM became the dominant tag management platform and is used on millions of websites. GDPR concerns emerged after 2018 because GTM facilitates easy deployment of tracking scripts, complicating cookie consent compliance.
Google Tag Manager.summary_abuse: Google Tag Manager has been exploited in supply chain attacks where attackers compromise GTM containers to inject malicious JavaScript on victim websites, including credit card skimmers (Magecart-style attacks). Because GTM allows arbitrary JavaScript execution in the browser, a compromised container can exfiltrate form data, session cookies, or payment details. Google provides container versioning and access controls, but organizations that fail to properly restrict GTM access are vulnerable to container hijacking.

Google Tag Manager for WordPress.history: Google Tag Manager for WordPress is a plugin developed by Thomas Geiger that simplifies adding Google Tag Manager to WordPress sites. Not an official Google product.
Google Tag Manager for WordPress.summary_abuse: Google Tag Manager for WordPress is a third-party WordPress plugin for GTM integration. GTM itself is frequently abused for data exfiltration on compromised sites by injecting tracking scripts. The plugin is not inherently malicious but requires careful permission management.

Google Wallet.history: Google Wallet launched in 2011 as Google's mobile payment service. It was merged with Android Pay in 2018 to form Google Pay. In 2022 Google rebranded Google Pay to Google Wallet in the US.
Google Wallet.summary_abuse: Google Wallet is targeted by payment fraud scams. Attackers impersonate the service to phish credentials or trick victims into sending money to fraudulent accounts. Google has anti-fraud measures in place.

Google Web Server.history: Google Web Server (GWS) is the proprietary HTTP server developed by Google, used to serve Google's own services. It is not publicly available and appears in HTTP response headers of Google-served pages.
Google Web Server.summary_abuse: Google Web Server is Google's internal web server infrastructure with no direct abuse vector for external parties.

Google Web Toolkit.history: Google Web Toolkit (GWT) was a Java-to-JavaScript compilation framework released by Google in 2006. Widely used for complex web applications. Google donated it to the GWT Project open-source community in 2019. It is now largely deprecated in favor of modern JavaScript frameworks.
Google Web Toolkit.summary_abuse: GWT is a deprecated Java-to-JavaScript development framework with no notable abuse signal.

Google Workspace.history: Google released Google Apps in 2006 as a free productivity suite, then launched a paid tier in 2007. In 2016 it was rebranded as G Suite to position itself as an office productivity platform. In 2020 it was renamed Google Workspace to signal its identity as a digital headquarters for teams. In January 2025 Google raised prices 17-22% across plans and bundled Gemini AI features into all Business and Enterprise tiers.
Google Workspace.summary_abuse: Google Workspace is broadly trusted enterprise software. Its Gmail component is occasionally used to send phishing or spam from compromised or trial accounts, but Google deploys sophisticated automated abuse detection and has fast takedown processes. The platform's sheer scale means some abuse occurs, but the ratio of abuse to legitimate use is extremely low and Google's anti-abuse response is among the best in the industry.

Goope.history: Goope is a Japanese website creation and homepage design service primarily serving the Japanese market; limited public history available.
Goope.summary_abuse: Goope is a Japanese homepage design system with no notable abuse signal documented. It serves small businesses in Japan creating their websites.

GoPrep.history: Founded in 2017 by Danny Ali and Mike Soldano in the USA as the first dedicated software for the meal prep and delivery industry; no notable acquisitions or ownership changes.
GoPrep.summary_abuse: GoPrep is a niche meal prep business management platform with no notable abuse signal. It manages operations for meal prep kitchens and does not offer public content hosting.

GOrendezvous.history: GOrendezvous is a Canadian online booking and scheduling platform founded around 2011, targeting health and wellness businesses. It allows businesses to accept online appointments and payments. The platform is primarily used in Quebec, Canada, and has grown to serve thousands of practitioners.
GOrendezvous.summary_abuse: GOrendezvous is a Canadian appointment booking platform for health and wellness businesses. No documented abuse incidents. Standard booking platform risks apply such as fake appointments.

Gorgias.history: Founded in 2015 by Romain Lapeyre and Alex Plugaru; Gorgias is a VC-backed ecommerce helpdesk that raised $104M total including $29M in 2024 at a $530M valuation with Shopify as a key investor.
Gorgias.summary_abuse: Gorgias is an ecommerce customer support helpdesk with no notable abuse signal. It manages merchant-to-customer support tickets and does not offer consumer-facing content hosting.

Gorilla Dash.history: Gorilla Dash is a multi-location management tool for franchises and businesses; limited public history available.
Gorilla Dash.summary_abuse: Gorilla Dash is a niche franchise management tool with no notable abuse signal documented.

Goshly.history: Goshly is a responsive website builder offering mobile and desktop site creation; limited public history available.
Goshly.summary_abuse: Goshly is a small website builder platform with no notable abuse signal documented.

GoSquared.history: GoSquared was founded in 2006 in London, UK and launched the first true real-time website analytics tool in 2009. The company has been profitable and independent for most of its existence, operating as a small team. It has expanded from analytics to include CRM, live chat, and marketing automation features. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
GoSquared.summary_abuse: GoSquared is a privacy-first web analytics and customer engagement platform for B2B SaaS. Its focus on privacy-respecting analytics and small team model means it has no notable abuse exposure. No documented incidents of misuse are known.

GoStats.history: GoStats is a free web traffic counter and analytics service that has been operating since approximately 1999, making it one of the older website hit counters on the internet. It provides basic visitor statistics via an embedded JavaScript counter. The service remains available but has minimal public information about its ownership or development team.
GoStats.summary_abuse: GoStats is a legacy website hit counter and analytics service. It provides basic visitor tracking and has no notable abuse patterns documented. Its age and limited technical capability mean it is not a significant target for abuse.

GotiPath.history: GotiPath was founded in 2016 in Dhaka, Bangladesh, becoming the country's first world-class cloud and CDN provider. The company partnered with Telekom Malaysia Berhad to co-develop CDN solutions and expand its network to 78+ points of presence globally. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes have occurred since founding.
GotiPath.summary_abuse: GotiPath is a CDN and cloud infrastructure provider with no notable abuse signal in public reporting. As an infrastructure provider serving primarily Bangladeshi and regional clients, it does not expose consumer-facing content hosting services that would attract phishing or scam operators at meaningful scale.

GOV.UK Elements.history: GOV.UK Elements was a CSS/HTML design patterns library created by the UK Government Digital Service around 2013 to help build consistent government services. It was superseded by GOV.UK Frontend in 2018, which provides a more modern, component-based approach. GOV.UK Elements is now officially deprecated and archived.
GOV.UK Elements.summary_abuse: GOV.UK Elements is a deprecated UK government CSS design library. As a frontend toolkit for building government websites, it has no meaningful abuse surface. No abuse incidents are documented.

GOV.UK Frontend.history: GOV.UK Frontend is the official frontend design system for UK government digital services, launched by the Government Digital Service in 2018 as a replacement for GOV.UK Elements and GOV.UK Template. It provides accessible, responsive HTML/CSS/JavaScript components for building government services. It is actively maintained and used across hundreds of UK government services.
GOV.UK Frontend.summary_abuse: GOV.UK Frontend is an open-source frontend toolkit created by the UK government. It has no consumer surface and no abuse history. It is used to build government web services and has no characteristics that would attract or enable abuse.

GOV.UK Template.history: GOV.UK Template was a Mustache/Jinja template library for UK government services, created by GDS circa 2013 to provide consistent header and footer styling. Deprecated in 2018 when GOV.UK Frontend replaced it with a more modern component-based approach. The repo is archived.
GOV.UK Template.summary_abuse: GOV.UK Template is a deprecated UK government HTML template library with no consumer-facing surface. No abuse history documented.

GOV.UK Toolkit.history: GOV.UK Frontend Toolkit was a Sass/CSS toolkit created by GDS circa 2013 to standardize styling across UK government digital services. It was superseded by GOV.UK Frontend in 2018 and is now deprecated and archived.
GOV.UK Toolkit.summary_abuse: GOV.UK Toolkit is a deprecated UK government frontend CSS library. No consumer surface, no abuse history.

Govalo.history: Govalo was founded in 2021 by Kelly Vaughn and Rhian Beutler as a Shopify app focused on digital gift cards and store credit for e-commerce merchants. The company is bootstrapped and operates independently within the Shopify app ecosystem.
Govalo.summary_abuse: Govalo is a Shopify gift card and store credit app serving e-commerce merchants. Gift card platforms can occasionally be abused for money laundering or fraud (purchasing gift cards with stolen payment methods), but Govalo operates within Shopify's ecosystem which enforces payment verification. No specific incidents or security reports have been documented for this vendor.

govCMS.history: govCMS is a Drupal-based open-source CMS platform initiated by the Australian Government in 2014 to provide a standardized and secure website platform for government agencies.
govCMS.summary_abuse: govCMS is an Australian government CMS platform with no notable abuse signal. It is used exclusively by government agencies.

GoVedia.history: GoVedia is a provider of custom ecommerce development solutions based in the US. The company offers tailored ecommerce builds for businesses. Limited public information is available about its founding history or team composition.
GoVedia.summary_abuse: GoVedia is a small ecommerce development firm. No documented abuse patterns are associated with the company. Its custom development model and limited online presence suggest minimal consumer-facing risk.

GoZen Forms.history: GoZen Forms is an AI-powered online form builder launched by GoZen, a bootstrapped SaaS startup based in India. It is part of the broader GoZen suite of marketing tools that includes GoZen Growth (email automation) and GoZen Notify (push notifications). The platform was designed to compete with tools like Typeform and SurveyMonkey, targeting small businesses and agencies with a freemium model and AI-assisted question generation.
GoZen Forms.summary_abuse: GoZen Forms has minimal documented abuse risk. As a form builder, it could theoretically be used to create phishing forms, though this is a risk common to all form-building tools. The vendor is a small Indian SaaS startup with limited documented AUP enforcement capacity. No specific abuse incidents have been publicly reported.

GPT AI Power.history: GPT AI Power is a WordPress plugin for AI content generation launched in early 2023, using OpenAI APIs to generate blog posts, product descriptions, and other content directly within WordPress. It gained significant traction as AI writing tools became popular, reaching over 10,000 active installations quickly.
GPT AI Power.summary_abuse: GPT AI Power is a WordPress AI content generation plugin. It can be used to mass-produce low-quality AI-written content for SEO spam or content farms, which is a notable potential abuse vector. The plugin relies on users providing their own OpenAI API keys, and the content generated depends entirely on how users deploy it.

Grab Pay Later.history: Grab Pay Later is a buy-now-pay-later feature within Grab's financial services platform, launched around 2018. Grab is a Southeast Asian superapp founded in 2012, listed on Nasdaq in 2021.
Grab Pay Later.summary_abuse: Grab Pay Later is a BNPL product from a regulated fintech in Southeast Asia. It has standard identity fraud and credit abuse exposure typical of BNPL services, with KYC processes and regulatory oversight.

Grade.us.history: Grade.us is a review management platform for local businesses and agencies founded around 2014 in the US.
Grade.us.summary_abuse: Grade.us is a review management SaaS with some potential for fake review generation, typical of this category. No significant abuse incidents documented.

Grafana.history: Grafana was created by Torkel Odegaard and first released in January 2014 as a fork of Kibana, designed to visualize time-series data from Graphite. Grafana Labs was founded in 2014 (originally as Raintank). The company received Series A funding of $24M in 2019, Series B of $50M in 2020, Series C of $220M in 2021 at a $3B valuation, and a $270M transaction in 2024 at a $6B valuation. Grafana grew into the LGTM stack (Loki, Grafana, Tempo, Mimir) and reported over $250M ARR by 2024.
Grafana.summary_abuse: Grafana has no significant documented abuse as a vector for malicious activity. It is primarily a backend observability and monitoring tool with no consumer-facing surface that bad actors would exploit. Vulnerabilities in Grafana (such as CVE-2021-43798, a path traversal flaw) have been exploited to read server files, but these are patched promptly. The self-hosted nature of most Grafana deployments means abuse is within the deploying organization's control.

Graffiti CMS.history: Graffiti CMS was a blog and content management system developed by Telligent Systems, released around 2007. It was later open-sourced and has been largely abandoned.
Graffiti CMS.summary_abuse: Graffiti CMS is an abandoned CMS. Sites running outdated installations without updates face elevated security risk from unpatched vulnerabilities.

Grain Harvest.history: Grain Harvest is an online data science tools and resources platform; limited public history available.
Grain Harvest.summary_abuse: Grain Harvest is a niche data science platform with no notable abuse signal documented.

GrandNode.history: GrandNode is an open-source multi-vendor e-commerce platform built on ASP.NET Core and MongoDB, founded around 2015 in Poland. It is a fork of nopCommerce adapted for MongoDB.
GrandNode.summary_abuse: GrandNode is an open-source e-commerce platform with no specific abuse pattern beyond generic e-commerce risks.

Granim.js.history: Granim.js is a JavaScript library for creating fluid and interactive gradient animations, released around 2017 under MIT license.
Granim.js.summary_abuse: Granim.js is a frontend animation library with no abuse surface.

GrapesJS.history: GrapesJS is an open-source web builder framework created by Artur Arseniev around 2016. It provides a drag-and-drop interface for building web pages and email templates.
GrapesJS.summary_abuse: GrapesJS is a web builder framework with no notable abuse signal.

GraphCMS.history: GraphCMS was founded in 2017 in Berlin, Germany as the first GraphQL-native headless CMS. The company raised a $10M Series A and then a $30M Series B in 2022 led by One Peak, bringing total funding to $43.7 million. On July 12, 2022, GraphCMS rebranded to Hygraph and launched a new federated content platform strategy, though the product remains the same service and is still commonly referenced under the original GraphCMS name.
GraphCMS.summary_abuse: GraphCMS (now Hygraph) is a headless CMS used by developers to manage content via GraphQL APIs. It has no notable abuse signal; its API-driven architecture and developer-facing nature do not attract phishing operators or fraud. The free tier requires account creation tied to an email address, limiting anonymous abuse.

Graphene.history: Graphene is a Python library for building GraphQL APIs, first released around 2015. It is the most popular GraphQL library for Python.
Graphene.summary_abuse: Graphene is a Python GraphQL library with no notable abuse signal.

GraphicsFlow.history: GraphicsFlow is a design and artwork management platform for decorated apparel businesses, founded around 2016 in the US.
GraphicsFlow.summary_abuse: GraphicsFlow is a niche B2B design management SaaS with no notable abuse signal.

Graphly.history: Graphly is a visual reporting and business intelligence SaaS platform founded around 2014, targeting SMBs needing KPI dashboards and metric visualization. No notable acquisitions or controversies documented.
Graphly.summary_abuse: Graphly is a B2B analytics tool with no known abuse signal. It has no consumer-facing content hosting or anonymous signup features that would attract misuse.

GraphQL.history: GraphQL is a data query and manipulation language for APIs developed by Facebook (Meta) and open-sourced in 2015. The specification and reference implementation were donated to the GraphQL Foundation under the Linux Foundation in 2018.
GraphQL.summary_abuse: GraphQL is an API specification standard with no inherent abuse vector. However, improperly secured GraphQL APIs are frequently targeted in attacks due to introspection exposure and batching abuse allowing data exfiltration.

Graphy.history: Graphy is a no-code content monetization platform founded around 2019, enabling creators to sell courses, memberships, and digital products. It raised funding and targeted the creator economy space.
Graphy.summary_abuse: Graphy is a creator economy platform with no notable abuse history. Standard payment and content policies apply.

Grappos.history: Grappos is a point-of-sale and order management SaaS for restaurants and retail, founded in the mid-2010s. No notable acquisitions or major incidents documented.
Grappos.summary_abuse: Grappos is a POS tool for merchants with no known abuse signal. Payment processing is typically done through established processors.

Grasp.history: Grasp is a customer support and helpdesk SaaS platform. Founded in the mid-2010s targeting small businesses. No significant ownership changes or controversies documented.
Grasp.summary_abuse: Grasp is a helpdesk tool with no notable abuse history. Standard SaaS with limited consumer-facing surface.

Grav.history: Grav CMS was created by Andy Miller and Rhuk (RocketTheme team) and first released in 2014 as a modern flat-file CMS built on PHP, Symfony components, Twig templating, and Markdown. It won Best Flat File CMS awards in 2017, 2019, 2020, and 2021. Grav was designed for developers who wanted a database-free CMS with version control-friendly file storage. It remains actively maintained as an open-source project under MIT license.
Grav.summary_abuse: Grav CMS has minimal abuse exposure. As a self-hosted flat-file CMS, any abuse depends on how site owners configure and deploy it. No documented large-scale exploitation campaigns target Grav specifically, though like all CMSs it is susceptible to vulnerabilities if not kept up to date. Several CVEs have been filed for Grav over the years (including XSS and file upload vulnerabilities) which are typically patched in updates.

Gravatar.history: Gravatar (Globally Recognized Avatar) was created by Tom Preston-Werner in 2004 and acquired by Automattic (the WordPress.com company) in 2007. It became the de-facto standard for user avatars across the web, used by millions of sites including WordPress, GitHub (historically), and countless forums. Automattic owns and operates Gravatar as a free service integrated into their broader ecosystem.
Gravatar.summary_abuse: Gravatar has been used in email enumeration attacks because querying an MD5 hash of any email address reveals whether that email has a Gravatar account. This privacy leak has been documented by security researchers. Despite Automattic's efforts to add privacy controls, the fundamental design enables email address verification at scale, which is occasionally exploited by spammers and scrapers. Automattic maintains a terms of service and responds to abuse reports.

Gravitec.history: Gravitec is a Ukrainian web push notification service founded around 2016, targeting publishers and e-commerce sites wanting to re-engage visitors. It competes with OneSignal and PushEngage.
Gravitec.summary_abuse: Web push notification services like Gravitec are frequently abused for spam and clickbait advertising. Malicious site operators use push notifications to bypass ad blockers and bombard users with unwanted notifications. Gravitec's free tier and easy signup potentially lower barriers for spammers, though the service publishes terms prohibiting spam. The push notification industry generally has a poor reputation for enabling intrusive advertising.

Gravito.history: Gravito is a Finnish consent management and first-party data platform founded around 2018. It focuses on GDPR-compliant audience data collection and consent orchestration for publishers.
Gravito.summary_abuse: Gravito is a consent management platform with no known abuse signal. It is designed to help sites comply with privacy regulations rather than circumvent them.

Gravity.history: Gravity Forms is a WordPress form plugin created by Rocketgenius, founded around 2008-2009. It became one of the most popular premium WordPress plugins with hundreds of thousands of active installations. The company has remained independent and developer-owned, releasing major updates regularly over 15+ years.
Gravity.summary_abuse: Gravity Forms is a WordPress plugin used to build contact forms and surveys. While forms can be used for phishing data collection if a site is compromised, Gravity Forms itself is not notably abused. The plugin's self-hosted nature means abuse depends on the site operator. The vendor maintains an active security team and patches vulnerabilities promptly.

Gravity Forms.history: Gravity Forms was developed by Rocketgenius and first released around 2008 as a premium WordPress form plugin. It became one of the most popular and well-regarded WordPress plugins due to its robust feature set including conditional logic, payment integrations, and a rich add-on ecosystem. Rocketgenius has maintained independent ownership throughout its history, making it a notable success story for a bootstrapped WordPress plugin company. It is used by millions of WordPress sites globally.
Gravity Forms.summary_abuse: Gravity Forms has a low abuse profile as a self-hosted WordPress plugin. Forms built with Gravity Forms have occasionally been targeted by automated spam bots submitting fake leads, which the plugin addresses through captcha integrations. The plugin's payment integrations (Stripe, PayPal) could theoretically be used to build fraudulent payment pages, but this is a risk common to all payment form builders. No systemic abuse campaigns targeting Gravity Forms specifically have been documented.

GreatPages.history: GreatPages is a landing page and website builder SaaS targeting small businesses and marketers. Founded in the early 2020s with limited public documentation of ownership history.
GreatPages.summary_abuse: Landing page builders like GreatPages can be used to create phishing pages or scam storefronts. No specific incidents documented for this vendor. Free or low-cost hosting of landing pages with custom domains raises moderate abuse risk typical of page builders.

Green Valley CMS.history: Green Valley CMS is a niche content management system with limited public documentation. It appears to target small organizations or municipalities needing a managed web platform.
Green Valley CMS.summary_abuse: Green Valley CMS has no notable abuse history. It is a niche CMS with a small user base and no known cases of it being used to facilitate fraud or malicious content.

Greenhouse.history: Greenhouse Software was founded in 2012 by Daniel Chait and Jon Stross in New York City. It raised significant VC funding and grew to become one of the leading applicant tracking systems for mid-market and enterprise companies. Greenhouse has remained independent and is used by thousands of companies for recruiting workflows.
Greenhouse.summary_abuse: Greenhouse is an enterprise ATS with no notable abuse history. It processes candidate personal data under strict enterprise contracts. The platform has no consumer-facing content hosting surface that would attract spam or fraud.

GreenRope.history: GreenRope is an all-in-one CRM, marketing automation, and sales platform founded in 2008 by Lars Helgeson in San Diego, California. It has remained an independent, bootstrapped company and is known for its flat-pricing model regardless of contact count.
GreenRope.summary_abuse: GreenRope is a CRM and email marketing platform with standard email sending capabilities. Like all email marketing tools, it could be misused for spam, but the company enforces anti-spam policies and its customer base is primarily small businesses. No notable abuse incidents documented.

GreenStory.history: GreenStory is a Canadian sustainability analytics and environmental impact reporting SaaS, founded around 2016. It targets brands wanting to calculate and communicate their environmental footprint to consumers.
GreenStory.summary_abuse: GreenStory is a sustainability data platform with no known abuse signal. It operates in a B2B context helping companies measure environmental impact.

GReminders.history: GReminders is a US-based appointment reminder service founded around 2016 that integrates with Google Calendar and Outlook to send automated SMS and email reminders. It targets healthcare providers, financial advisors, and service businesses. No notable acquisitions or controversies are documented.
GReminders.summary_abuse: GReminders is an appointment reminder tool that sends SMS/email reminders. Like all messaging automation platforms, it could potentially be misused for spam, but the service requires calendar integration and targets legitimate professional services. No documented abuse incidents.

Greyd.Suite.history: Greyd.Suite is a German WordPress page builder and design suite, founded around 2019. It targets German-speaking web professionals wanting a GDPR-friendly alternative to US-based builders. The company is independent and small.
Greyd.Suite.summary_abuse: Greyd.Suite is a WordPress plugin with no known abuse history. It is primarily used by German web agencies and has no consumer-facing attack surface.

Griddo.history: Griddo is a Digital Experience Platform built specifically for higher education institutions, developed by a Spanish company. It offers a no-code headless CMS that allows university marketing and communications teams to manage multiple websites, faculties, and landing pages without IT dependency. The platform is built on JavaScript with React and Node. It targets the specific niche of European universities needing multi-site governance.
Griddo.summary_abuse: Griddo is a niche enterprise CMS targeting higher education institutions and has no documented abuse exposure. The platform's enterprise-only sales model and focus on institutional clients means it is unlikely to be involved in consumer-facing scam or spam activity.

Gridsome.history: Gridsome is an open-source Vue.js static site generator inspired by Gatsby, created by Tommy and Hans-Jørgen Vedvik from Norway in 2018. Development slowed significantly after 2021 as Nuxt.js and other frameworks grew, leaving the project largely dormant.
Gridsome.summary_abuse: Gridsome is a static site generator with no abuse signal. It has no hosted platform or runtime execution surface that could be exploited for malicious purposes.

Gridster.history: Gridster.js is an open-source jQuery plugin for building draggable grid layouts, created by Ducksboard around 2012. Development largely ceased after Ducksboard was acquired by New Relic in 2014, leaving the library effectively abandoned with no significant updates since.
Gridster.summary_abuse: Gridster.js is a client-side UI library with no abuse signal. It has no server component or hosted service.

Grin.history: GRIN is an influencer marketing SaaS founded in 2014 in Sacramento, California. It raised over $110 million in VC funding and positioned itself as a leading creator management platform for DTC brands. The company has remained independent and grown significantly in the influencer marketing space.
Grin.summary_abuse: GRIN is an influencer marketing platform with standard social media data handling. Marketing platforms can facilitate influencer fraud through fake follower metrics, though GRIN has tools to detect such fraud. No notable security incidents or regulatory actions documented.

Grivy.history: Grivy is a referral and loyalty marketing platform for e-commerce, founded in the early 2020s. Limited public information about the company's ownership history or funding.
Grivy.summary_abuse: Grivy is a referral marketing tool with limited abuse history. Referral programs can be exploited for coupon fraud, but no specific incidents involving this vendor are documented.

GrocerKey.history: GrocerKey is a white-label grocery e-commerce platform founded around 2014, providing online ordering and delivery infrastructure for independent grocery retailers and supermarkets. It competes with Instacart's white-label offering and similar solutions.
GrocerKey.summary_abuse: GrocerKey is a B2B grocery e-commerce platform with no notable abuse history. It processes consumer orders for grocery retailers with standard payment security requirements.

Groobee.history: Groobee is a South Korean e-commerce personalization and product recommendation platform, founded around 2014. It targets online retailers with AI-powered recommendation engines and behavioral analytics.
Groobee.summary_abuse: Groobee is a recommendation engine with no known abuse history. It collects behavioral data for personalization, which is standard for e-commerce analytics tools.

Groove.history: Groove (GrooveHQ) was founded in 2011 by Alex Turnbull, inspired by his frustrations with bloated help desk software during a previous venture. The company grew organically without significant venture funding, reaching over $10M in ARR. It operates under the OptimizeCX umbrella which includes related products Helply and InstantDocs. The WPBeginner Fund (Syed Balki) made an investment in Groove to support continued product development.
Groove.summary_abuse: Groove has no documented abuse exposure. It is a B2B helpdesk SaaS used by small businesses for customer support, with no consumer-facing signup or hosting features that would attract bad actors. The platform processes customer support emails which are low-risk from an abuse perspective.

Groove.cm.history: Groove.cm (originally GrooveFunnels) is an all-in-one marketing SaaS launched in 2020 by Mike Filsaime and John Cornetta. It offered a freemium funnel builder and marketing suite. The platform received criticism for its aggressive launch tactics, delayed feature delivery, and controversies over its lifetime deal sales. The product has been rebranded and continues operating.
Groove.cm.summary_abuse: Groove.cm has faced criticism for aggressive email marketing practices and dark patterns in its sales funnel, including high-pressure lifetime deal tactics. As a platform enabling users to build sales pages and marketing funnels, it has the same abuse risks as other funnel builders. No significant fraud or phishing incidents directly attributed to the platform.

GrooveKart.history: GrooveKart is an e-commerce platform launched by Mike Filsaime and John Cornetta under the Groove Digital umbrella around 2019, positioned as a Shopify alternative. It gained initial traction through aggressive affiliate marketing. The platform has faced criticism for overpromising features.
GrooveKart.summary_abuse: GrooveKart enables users to set up online stores. Like other e-commerce platforms, it can host fraudulent storefronts and counterfeit goods sellers. The platform's lenient onboarding and affiliate-heavy marketing have contributed to some criticism in the e-commerce community.

GroovePages.history: GroovePages is a drag-and-drop funnel and landing page builder launched by Mike Filsaime and John Cornetta of Groove Digital in 2019-2020. It was marketed aggressively to affiliate marketers and was part of the GrooveFunnels (now Groove.cm) suite. The platform attracted controversy over its lifetime deal marketing and delayed feature launches.
GroovePages.summary_abuse: GroovePages is used to build marketing funnels and landing pages. Like all funnel builders with free tiers, it can be exploited to create phishing pages or fraudulent storefronts. The platform has been popular with aggressive affiliate marketers. No specific mass phishing campaigns have been attributed to the platform.

GroupBy.history: GroupBy Inc. is a Canadian e-commerce search, merchandising, and personalization platform founded in 2013 in Toronto. It raised VC funding and serves enterprise retailers, integrating with Google Cloud's retail AI capabilities. The company has remained independent.
GroupBy.summary_abuse: GroupBy is an enterprise e-commerce search platform with no known abuse history. It operates behind-the-scenes for retail sites with no consumer-facing attack surface.

Growave.history: Growave (originally SocialShopWave) is a Shopify app providing loyalty programs, wishlists, reviews, and social login, founded in 2014 in Kyrgyzstan. It rebranded to Growave and grew to serve thousands of Shopify stores as an all-in-one social marketing tool.
Growave.summary_abuse: Growave is a Shopify loyalty and review app with no notable abuse history. It collects customer data for merchants within standard Shopify data governance rules.

Growcer.history: Growcer is a white-label grocery marketplace software product by FATbit Technologies, an Indian software development company. Launched around 2020, it targets entrepreneurs wanting to build Instacart-like grocery delivery platforms. FATbit also makes Yo!Kart and similar marketplace scripts.
Growcer.summary_abuse: Growcer is a self-hosted marketplace script with no direct abuse history. As a white-label product sold to operators, security and compliance are the operator's responsibility. FATbit's products have had known vulnerabilities in the past per third-party security audits.

Growform.history: Growform is a multi-step form builder focused on lead generation, founded around 2020. It targets marketing agencies and performance marketers seeking higher lead conversion rates through multi-step form psychology. The platform gained traction via AppSumo lifetime deals and is used by companies like Stax Payments. It is backed by Cloudflare infrastructure for global form delivery.
Growform.summary_abuse: Growform is a multi-step form builder primarily used for lead generation. As with all form builders, it could be used to create phishing forms or fake lead capture pages, though this is not a documented pattern specific to Growform. The platform focuses on legitimate marketing use cases and serves well-known clients. No specific abuse incidents have been publicly reported.

Growing Good.history: Growing Good Technology Ltd was founded in 2022 and is registered in Oxford, UK (company number 14017010). It was built by a team with over 10 years of combined veg-box industry experience, creating a purpose-built SaaS platform for UK veg box schemes and subscription food businesses to manage operations, subscriptions, and sales.
Growing Good.summary_abuse: Growing Good is a niche B2B SaaS platform serving UK veg box and farm subscription businesses. It has no notable abuse profile, no consumer-facing anonymous signup, and serves a small specialised market segment where abuse risk is minimal.

GrowingIO.history: GrowingIO is a Chinese digital analytics and marketing intelligence platform founded in 2015 by Zhang Xu in Beijing. It raised significant VC funding and became one of China's leading analytics platforms serving enterprises. The company focuses on behavior analytics and user journey tracking.
GrowingIO.summary_abuse: GrowingIO is a Chinese analytics platform primarily used by enterprises. Being based in China raises data sovereignty concerns for Western users given China's data access laws. No specific fraud or phishing incidents documented. The tracker density is moderate given the behavioral analytics functionality.

GrowSurf.history: GrowSurf is a developer-centric referral program SaaS founded in 2019, targeting B2B SaaS companies with technical integrations via API. It competes with Viral Loops and ReferralHero. The company is bootstrapped or lightly funded and has remained independent.
GrowSurf.summary_abuse: GrowSurf powers referral programs for B2B SaaS, which can be exploited for referral fraud. No specific abuse incidents documented. Standard anti-fraud measures are expected in any referral system.

GrowthBook.history: GrowthBook was founded around 2020 and went through Y Combinator. It launched as an open-source feature flagging and A/B testing platform, positioning itself as a warehouse-native alternative to commercial tools like LaunchDarkly and Optimizely. The project attracted over 3,000 companies as users. The open-core model uses MIT for the bulk of the codebase with a commercial license for enterprise features.
GrowthBook.summary_abuse: GrowthBook is a developer-focused feature flagging and experimentation platform with no meaningful documented abuse exposure. The tool is used for internal product development workflows and has no consumer-facing surface that would attract bad actors. Self-hosted deployments put security entirely in the hands of the deploying organization.

Gruvi.history: Gruvi is a Belgian OTT video streaming and fan engagement platform founded around 2014, targeting sports clubs, media companies, and entertainment brands. The company provides white-label streaming apps and subscription management.
Gruvi.summary_abuse: Gruvi is a B2B video streaming platform with no notable abuse history. It provides infrastructure for content owners and has no anonymous consumer content hosting that would attract misuse.

GSAP.history: GSAP (GreenSock Animation Platform) was created by Jack Doyle beginning around 2008 as a solution for efficient Flash banner animations. It evolved from TweenLite into a comprehensive JavaScript animation suite. In October 2024, Webflow acquired the GreenSock business, after which all previously premium plugins became free. On April 30, 2025, GSAP became fully free for all users including commercial use.
GSAP.summary_abuse: GSAP is a JavaScript animation library with no meaningful abuse profile. It is a pure front-end rendering library with no server component, no user data collection, and no consumer-facing sign-up surface. Abuse risk is negligible.

GTranslate.history: GTranslate was founded around 2008 in Armenia as a website translation service using Google Translate. It grew to serve over 500,000 websites, offering automatic and human-assisted translation. The service became one of the most widely used website translation plugins for WordPress and other CMS platforms.
GTranslate.summary_abuse: GTranslate provides website translation using Google Translate. The free tier used by many sites uses Google's translation infrastructure. Privacy concerns exist as translated page requests are proxied through GTranslate's servers. Occasionally used on low-quality content sites, but no major documented abuse incidents.

GTranslate app.history: GTranslate app refers to the Shopify and other e-commerce app versions of GTranslate's website translation service. Built on GTranslate's core infrastructure from 2008, the app integrations were added as e-commerce platforms grew in popularity. Same Armenian company behind the main GTranslate service.
GTranslate app.summary_abuse: GTranslate app is the e-commerce integration version of GTranslate's website translation service. Same characteristics as the main product - proxied translations through GTranslate servers, potential privacy concerns, but no major documented abuse incidents.

GuardFlame.history: GuardFlame appears to be a small web security or firewall product with limited public documentation. Founded in the early 2020s with minimal market presence.
GuardFlame.summary_abuse: GuardFlame is a security tool with no documented abuse history. Security tools are generally not exploited for abusive purposes by their operators.

Guest Suite.history: Guest Suite is a French customer review and satisfaction management platform founded around 2016, specializing in hospitality and retail sectors. It aggregates reviews from multiple platforms and provides reputation management tools.
Guest Suite.summary_abuse: Guest Suite is a review management platform with no known abuse history. It helps businesses collect and respond to customer feedback within standard GDPR compliance frameworks.

Guestonline.history: Guestonline is a French restaurant reservation and table management SaaS founded around 2012. It serves restaurant operators in France and Europe with booking management tools. The company has remained independent.
Guestonline.summary_abuse: Guestonline is a restaurant reservation platform with no known abuse history. It processes customer booking data within GDPR requirements.

Guesty.history: Guesty is an Israeli property management software company founded in 2013 by Amiad and Koby Soto for professional Airbnb hosts. It has raised over $170 million in VC funding and grown into a leading platform for short-term rental property managers worldwide, handling multi-channel listings and guest communication.
Guesty.summary_abuse: Guesty is used by short-term rental property managers. The short-term rental industry has experienced issues with fraud including fake listings and payment scams, though these originate from end-users on platforms like Airbnb rather than from Guesty itself. Guesty has no significant direct abuse history.

Guidap.history: Guidap is a French booking and reservation management platform for outdoor activity and experience providers, founded around 2018. It targets sports clubs, escape rooms, and tour operators.
Guidap.summary_abuse: Guidap is a booking platform for activity providers with no known abuse history. It operates in a B2B context within GDPR compliance.

GuideIT.history: GuideIT appears to be a Swedish IT service management or helpdesk SaaS with limited public documentation. Founded in the mid-2010s with a small market presence.
GuideIT.summary_abuse: GuideIT is an IT management platform with no known abuse history. It serves B2B customers and has no consumer-facing content hosting surface.

GuildQuality.history: GuildQuality is a customer satisfaction survey platform founded in 2006 by Aaron Hirschi in Atlanta, GA, specializing in the home building and renovation industry. The company was acquired by Porch Group (PRCH), a publicly traded home services platform, around 2021.
GuildQuality.summary_abuse: GuildQuality is a survey and review platform for home service contractors with no notable abuse history. It is backed by the public company Porch Group.

GumGum.history: GumGum is an advertising technology company founded in 2008 by Ophir Tanz in Santa Monica, California. It pioneered in-image and contextual advertising using computer vision. The company raised significant VC funding and became a major player in the contextual ad industry, particularly as privacy regulations prompted a shift away from behavioral tracking. GumGum has remained independent.
GumGum.summary_abuse: GumGum is an ad-tech company whose scripts appear on millions of websites. Ad networks are frequently used to deliver malvertisements, and GumGum has been implicated in industry-wide ad fraud concerns typical for the sector. The company employs contextual targeting rather than behavioral tracking, reducing some privacy concerns compared to cookie-based competitors. It is a member of IAB and TAG (Trustworthy Accountability Group).

Gumlet.history: Gumlet is an Indian media delivery platform founded in 2018, offering image optimization CDN and video streaming services. It raised seed funding and grew to serve thousands of publishers. The company is based in Bangalore with global CDN infrastructure.
Gumlet.summary_abuse: Gumlet is a media delivery CDN with no significant abuse history. CDNs can be used to host and distribute malicious content, but Gumlet's primary customers are publishers and developers. Its infrastructure is not known for being exploited for phishing or malware delivery.

Gumroad.history: Gumroad was founded in 2011 by Sahil Lavingia (formerly of Pinterest). The company raised VC funding and built a platform for creators to sell digital products. Gumroad famously laid off most of its staff in 2015 after a failed Series B raise, becoming an extremely lean company. Sahil later wrote publicly about the experience. The company recovered and grew, eventually shifting to an open-source approach for parts of its codebase.
Gumroad.summary_abuse: Gumroad enables anyone to sell digital products with minimal verification. It has been used to sell pirated content, scam products, and low-quality AI-generated materials. The free tier and easy signup lower barriers for bad actors. Gumroad investigates reports of fraud and removes violating listings, but the scale of use means some abuse persists. Payment fraud and refund abuse are also documented concerns from sellers.

Gumstack.history: Gumstack appears to be a small e-commerce or creator storefront builder founded in the early 2020s with limited public documentation and market presence.
Gumstack.summary_abuse: Gumstack is a small e-commerce platform with no documented abuse history. Low vendor stability reduces trust in security practices.

gunicorn.history: Gunicorn (Green Unicorn) is a Python WSGI HTTP server inspired by Ruby's Unicorn, first released around 2010 by Benoit Chesneau; it became one of the most widely used Python web server gateways, particularly popular for deploying Django and Flask applications.
gunicorn.summary_abuse: Gunicorn is a Python WSGI server with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal; it is a backend infrastructure component used to deploy Python web applications.

GuppY.history: GuppY is a French open-source CMS created by Dominique Ottello around 2003, designed to be easy to install without a database. It gained modest popularity in the French-speaking community. Development has been largely dormant since the mid-2010s.
GuppY.summary_abuse: GuppY is an outdated French CMS with minimal modern usage. Its aging codebase may contain unpatched vulnerabilities, and abandoned installations could be compromised. No specific abuse campaigns attributed to it.

Gurucan.history: Gurucan is an online course, membership, and community platform founded around 2018, targeting coaches, educators, and content creators. It raised seed funding and competes with Teachable, Kajabi, and similar platforms.
Gurucan.summary_abuse: Gurucan is a course platform that could be used to sell scam or low-quality educational products. Creator platforms generally face challenges with course fraud. No specific incidents documented for this vendor.

Gutenberg.history: Gutenberg is the block-based editor for WordPress, officially launched on December 6, 2018 with WordPress 5.0, replacing the TinyMCE-based Classic Editor. It was developed by Automattic and the WordPress open-source community, named after Johannes Gutenberg. Full Site Editing was introduced in WordPress 5.8 in 2021 and became a core feature in WordPress 5.9 in 2022. The project is dual-licensed under GPL v2 and MPL v2.
Gutenberg.summary_abuse: Gutenberg as a WordPress block editor plugin has a minimal direct abuse profile. WordPress-based sites broadly are targeted by attackers, but Gutenberg itself is not specifically exploited more than the broader WordPress ecosystem. The plugin is distributed through the official WordPress repository with code review, and the WordPress security team has a public responsible disclosure policy.

Guuru.history: Guuru is a Swiss SaaS company founded around 2015 offering peer-to-peer customer support via a community chat feature. It enables brands to connect customers with existing expert users for product support, reducing call center load.
Guuru.summary_abuse: Guuru is a peer support SaaS with no known abuse history. It operates within standard GDPR data requirements and serves B2B customers.

GX WebManager.history: GX WebManager is a web content management product from GeneXus, a Uruguayan software company. GeneXus has been developing low-code development tools since 1984. GX WebManager is a CMS module within the GeneXus ecosystem targeted at enterprise web management, particularly in Latin American markets.
GX WebManager.summary_abuse: GX WebManager is an enterprise CMS product from GeneXus with no documented abuse history. As an enterprise B2B tool used mainly in Latin America for government and corporate web management, it presents minimal abuse risk.
